v.t: Pope Innocent III calls for the Fourth Crusade
v.s: Since his accession in 1198, Pope Innocent III had been committed to restoring papal supremacy in Christendom. In 1201, at the Lateran Palace, he issued a call to the knights of Europe to launch a new crusade to recover Jerusalem. The appeal attracted a large number of knights from France, Germany and Italy, but the lack of unified command and sufficient transport ships caused the subsequent campaign to deviate from its original goal, eventually leading to the tragedy of the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople in 1202-1204.

The seven-volume 'Zhenjiu Zisheng Jing' (Classic of Acupuncture and Moxibustion for Life Nourishment), compiled by the Song physician Wang Zhizhong over more than a decade, was printed in Lin'an (present-day Hangzhou). The work summarized pre-Song achievements in acupuncture, recording 365 acupoints with detailed information on location, indications, moxibustion techniques, and emphasized clinical experience with point pressure. This classic significantly influenced later acupuncture development and remains an important reference in TCM.

The Fourth Crusade, called by Pope Innocent III, originally aimed to attack the Ayyubid Sultanate in Egypt as a base to recover Jerusalem. The crusaders assembled in Venice but could not pay for the ships provided by the Venetians; they were forced to accept the terms of Doge Enrico Dandolo to help Venice capture the Christian city of Zara (present-day Zadar, Croatia). In November 1202 the crusaders captured Zara, and the pope excommunicated all of them. The crusade completely deviated from its original goal of reconquering the Holy Land.

To strengthen administration over the Shandong Peninsula, Emperor Zhangzong of Jin elevated Yidu Prefecture of Shandong East Circuit to a general headquarters, governing surrounding counties and prefectures. Its seat was at present-day Qingzhou, Shandong, making it an important administrative and military center in the east. The move aimed to consolidate Jin control over Han-populated areas and guard against possible Song naval attacks.

v.t: First siege of Constantinople by crusaders
v.s: The Fourth Crusade, drawn into Byzantine dynastic struggles due to Venetian commercial rivalry, was induced to intervene. The exiled Byzantine prince Alexios IV promised 200,000 silver marks and submission of the Orthodox Church to the pope in exchange for help regaining his throne. In July 1203 the crusaders breached parts of Constantinople, forcing Emperor Alexios III to flee. Alexios IV and his father Isaac II were crowned together, but their relationship with the crusaders rapidly deteriorated as he could not fulfill his promises.

v.t: Minamoto no Yoriie establishes shogunate regency system
v.s: The second shogun of the Kamakura shogunate, Minamoto no Yoriie, was physically weak and inept at governing, so his maternal grandfather Hōjō Tokimasa and son Hōjō Yoshitoki gradually took actual power. In September 1203 the Hōjō clan created the post of 'shikken' (regent), with Hōjō Yoshitoki as its holder, to assist the shogun in handling affairs. This marked the beginning of the Hōjō overshadowing the shogun's authority, laying the foundation for the shikken to become the de facto supreme ruler of the shogunate.

The Fourth Crusade clashed with Alexios V, son of the deposed Alexios III. On 12 April 1204 the crusaders launched a general assault, broke through the walls of Constantinople, and then sacked the ancient city for three days, looting countless precious artifacts and religious relics, including the bronze doors and golden statues of Hagia Sophia. The Byzantine Empire was overthrown, and the crusaders established the Latin Empire (1204-1261). The sack caused an irreparable rift between the Eastern and Western churches and permanently weakened Byzantium as the eastern bulwark of Christendom.

After the sack of Constantinople, the crusader leaders and the Venetians jointly partitioned Byzantine territory and established the Latin Empire. Baldwin I, Count of Flanders, was elected emperor, and the Republic of Venice received three-eighths of Byzantine territory, including Crete and important ports in the Peloponnese. The Latin Empire nominally continued the Byzantine tradition, but its effective control was limited to Constantinople and its immediate surroundings; Greek nobles set up exile regimes, the most powerful being the Empire of Nicaea, the Despotate of Epirus, and the Empire of Trebizond.

After years of war and legal battles, King Philip II Augustus of France seized all of the Duchy of Normandy from King John of England. In 1204 French forces captured Rouen, the Norman capital, and the heartland of the Angevin Empire on the continent was incorporated into the royal demesne. This marked a dramatic strengthening of French royal power, leaving England with only a remnant of Aquitaine in the south, and the territorial disputes became a source of conflict for decades.

v.t: Empress Dowager Chen regent of Vietnam's Ly dynasty
v.s: When Emperor Gaozong of the Vietnamese Ly dynasty died, the crown prince Li Sam (Li Hue Tong) succeeded as Emperor Hue Tong. Because he was young and sickly, Empress Dowager Chen (daughter of Chen Li) acted as regent under the title 'Zhaosheng Empress Dowager', holding actual power. She relied heavily on her elder brother Chen Siqing, gradually handing the court over to the Chen clan. This paved the way for the Chen family to replace the Ly and establish the Tran dynasty, marking a major power transition in Vietnamese history.

v.t: Establishment of the Empire of Nicaea
v.s: After the fall of Constantinople, the Byzantine noble Theodore I Laskaris fled to Nicaea (present-day Iznik, Turkey) and, with the support of local Greek nobles and the Orthodox Church, established the Empire of Nicaea. This regime regarded itself as the legitimate heir of the Byzantine Empire and quickly took in scholars, officials and generals who had fled Constantinople. Theodore I then launched campaigns to recover large parts of northwestern Asia Minor, becoming the core force that would later restore Constantinople.

v.t: Completion of Notre-Dame de Paris west facade
v.s: After more than forty years of construction (begun 1163), the iconic twin-tower west facade of Notre-Dame de Paris and its three deeply recessed pointed arches (the portals of the Last Judgment, the Virgin, and St. Anne) were largely completed. The west rose window, 10 meters in diameter, features stained glass depicting biblical stories and the life of the Virgin. The Gothic style's flying buttresses and pointed arches gave the interior a height of 32.5 meters, making it one of the tallest buildings in Europe at the time and greatly influencing later European church architecture.

v.t: Southern Song minister Han Tuozhou plans northern expedition
v.s: To consolidate his political position and crush political opponents such as the Daoxue faction, Southern Song powerful minister Han Tuozhou began secretly preparing a northern expedition against the Jin. He gathered generals for consultations at his mansion in Lin'an and instructed clients to write a denunciation of the Jin, using the slogan 'recover the Central Plains'. Emperor Ningzong, influenced by Han, leaned toward war, but the Song army was poorly trained and equipment was lacking; even the minister of revenue pointed out that the treasury could not support a large-scale war. Han ignored the opposition and accelerated preparations.

v.t: Genghis Khan establishes the Mongol Empire
v.s: At a kurultai convened at the source of the Onon River, the Mongol tribes elected Temüjin as 'Genghis Khan', meaning 'Khan of the Oceans' or 'Universal Ruler'. He promulgated the 'Great Yassa' (Mongol code of laws) and established a strict military-civilian 'thousand-unit' system: the entire Mongol population was divided into 95 thousand-units, each under a hereditary commander from among his founding generals. He also created the kheshig (imperial guard), selecting 10,000 sons of nobles to form an elite guard that both protected the khan and served as a training ground for senior commanders. The establishment of the Mongol Empire fundamentally altered the subsequent course of Eurasian history.

v.t: Outbreak of the Kaixi Northern Expedition
v.s: In May, without adequate preparation, Southern Song minister Han Tuozhou launched a three-pronged northern expedition against the Jin. The eastern army under Guo Ni attacked Suzhou (present-day Suzhou, Anhui), the central army under Li Shuang attacked Caizhou (present-day Runan, Henan), and the western army under Huangfu Bin attacked Tangzhou (present-day Biyang, Henan). Initial Song successes occurred because the Jin were caught off guard, but then the Jin concentrated elite forces and counterattacked; the Song suffered repeated defeats due to supply shortages and command errors. The Jin then invaded Song territory, and the Kaixi Northern Expedition ended in disastrous defeat.

Qutb ud-Din Aibak, a former Turkic slave general of the Ghurid dynasty, declared an independent regime at Delhi after the assassination of the Ghurid sultan Muhammad, establishing the Delhi Sultanate (also known as the Mamluk or Slave dynasty, 1206-1290). Qutb ud-Din made Delhi his capital, where he built the Qutb Minar (Victory Tower) and the first large mosque. The Delhi Sultanate marked the beginning of over three centuries of Islamic rule in North India, profoundly influencing the subcontinent's religion, culture, and politics.

The Italian friar Francis of Assisi (1181-1226) founded the Order of Friars Minor (Franciscans) in 1209, initially with only twelve members. Francis advocated absolute poverty; the friars lived by begging and preached the gospel everywhere. In 1209 Francis went to Rome to meet Pope Innocent III, who, despite some reservations about the strict poverty rule, gave formal approval to the Franciscan rule. The order expanded rapidly in the following decades, becoming one of the most important mendicant orders in Catholicism.

v.t: Zagwe dynasty builds rock-hewn churches of Lalibela
v.s: King Lalibela, the most famous ruler of the Zagwe dynasty in Ethiopia, ascended the throne. He ordered the carving of eleven monolithic rock-hewn churches in the red volcanic rock of the capital Roha (now Lalibela) to symbolize a 'New Jerusalem'. The churches were carved from the top down; the largest, the House of the Saviour, is over 10 meters high and features columns, altars, and baptismal pools. The Lalibela churches later became a holy site of Ethiopian Orthodoxy and one of Africa's most remarkable medieval architectural wonders.

v.t: Shi Miyuan stages coup, kills Han Tuozhou
v.s: After the failure of the Kaixi Northern Expedition, the Jin demanded that the Song cede the Huai River region and hand over the head of chief war hawk Han Tuozhou. Although Emperor Ningzong was reluctant to kill Han, the anti-Han forces at court, led by Vice Minister of Rites Shi Miyuan and Empress Yang, conspired and on 24 November intercepted Han on his way to court, killing him inside the Yujin Garden. Shi Miyuan then sent Han's head to the Jin to sue for peace, and he himself was promoted to Right Grand Chancellor and Commissioner of Military Affairs, monopolizing court power for 26 years.

In 1207, Genghis Khan sent his eldest son Jochi with a Mongol army to conquer the Kirghiz tribes (ancestors of the Kyrgyz people) living in the upper Yenisei River region, who lived by nomadism and hunting. When Jochi's army approached, the Kirghiz chief surrendered without a fight, offering white falcons and other precious gifts to Genghis Khan. This campaign was an important step in the Mongol Empire's northwest expansion, laying the foundation for later conquests of Siberian forest tribes and opening the trade route to Central Asia.

Under Shi Miyuan, the Southern Song concluded a peace treaty with the Jin: the annual Song tribute of 200,000 taels of silver and 200,000 bolts of silk was increased to 300,000 each, plus an indemnity of 3 million taels of silver; the Jin withdrew from occupied Song territory; the two states changed their relationship to uncle-nephew (Jin as uncle, Song as nephew). The Song also sent the heads of Han Tuozhou and Su Shidan to the Jin. The Treaty of Jiading further burdened the Southern Song's finances but gave over a decade of relative peace.

Pope Innocent III launched the Albigensian Crusade to eradicate the Cathar heresy (also called Albigensians) in Languedoc in southern France. He called on the knights of northern France to march south, promising the same indulgences as a pilgrimage to the Holy Land. On 22 July the crusaders captured Béziers, the Cathar center, and massacred about 20,000 inhabitants (including many Catholics). Under the command of papal legate Arnaud Amalric, who reportedly said 'Kill them all; God will know his own,' the crusaders ruthlessly suppressed the Cathars for twenty years.

v.t: Philip II issues the Judicial Reform Ordinance
v.s: King Philip II of France issued a judicial reform ordinance that made all judicial cases in the royal domain subject to the royal court, stripping feudal lords of most of their judicial powers. The ordinance also abolished trial by combat and encouraged written evidence and witness testimony. This reform greatly strengthened royal authority, weakened local feudal powers, and laid the foundation for a unified central judicial system in France.

v.t: Foundation of the University of Cambridge
v.s: Some Oxford scholars, after a violent conflict with local townspeople in which they killed two women, faced reprisals from the townsfolk. To escape trial by the Oxford county court, a group of scholars fled to Cambridge, settling by the River Cam and establishing the University of Cambridge. They initially taught in St Mary's Church, gradually forming an independent academic community. Later, in 1231, King Henry III granted Cambridge a monopoly on teaching, making it the second university in the English-speaking world after Oxford.

v.t: First Mongol campaign against Western Xia
v.s: Genghis Khan launched his first campaign against Western Xia to punish it for refusing to send troops to aid the Mongols against the Jin and to open a western route for attacking the Jin. The Mongol army advanced swiftly, reaching the Western Xia capital Zhongxing (present-day Yinchuan, Ningxia). Emperor Xiangzong of Western Xia was forced to give a daughter in marriage, agree to become a Mongol vassal, and pledge troops for future attacks on the Jin. However, Western Xia's submission was only temporary, and war between the two sides continued for decades.

The Spanish canon Domingo de Guzmán founded the Order of Preachers (Dominicans) in Toulouse, France. The order emphasized learning and preaching; its members were mostly well-educated, dedicated to spreading orthodox doctrine in cities where heresy was rampant. The Dominicans quickly received papal approval from Innocent III and later dominated the operation of the medieval Inquisition, producing such famous theologians as Thomas Aquinas.

v.t: Prelude to the Kamakura shogunate's 'Goseibai Shikimoku'
v.s: To resolve frequent land disputes and debt problems among the gokenin (shogunal vassals), Kamakura regent Hōjō Yoshitoki began organizing legal experts to compile a written code. This work took many years, culminating in the 1232 promulgation of the 51-article 'Goseibai Shikimoku', Japan's first warrior code. Around 1209 the Hōjō had already started collecting case precedents, laying the foundation for the later code.

Shams ud-Din Iltutmish, the third sultan of the Mamluk dynasty, came to power. Originally a slave of Qutb al-Din Aibak, he distinguished himself through military and political talent. Iltutmish consolidated the Delhi Sultanate's rule in northern India, crushed Rajput revolts, and expanded its territory to Bengal and Sindh. He also completed the Qutb Minar in Delhi and established a system of sultanic succession, laying the foundations for the sultanate's stable rule.

During their conquest of Central Asia, the Mongols captured many Muslim artisans, some of whom knew how to make counterweight trebuchets (called 'huihuipao' or 'Muslim trebuchet'). Around 1210, this technology began to reach the Southern Song border. Compared to the traditional torsion trebuchets, the counterweight trebuchet could throw heavier stones over longer distances. It became a crucial siege weapon in the subsequent Song-Mongol wars, playing a decisive role in the 1273 battle of Xiangyang-Fancheng.

When Genghis Khan conquered the Naiman tribe, he captured their seal keeper, Tata-tonga, and ordered him to create a script for the Mongols based on the Uyghur alphabet. Tata-tonga slightly modified the 22 letters of the Uyghur script to suit the phonetic features of the Mongolian language, completing the task around 1210. This Uyghur-based script became the official script of the Mongol Empire, used for decrees, histories, and letters. After further modifications, it has survived to the present day as the precursor of modern Mongolian script.

v.t: Paris teachers' guild receives royal charter
v.s: King Philip II of France granted a royal charter to the guild of teachers and students of Paris, confirming its independent jurisdiction and right to suspend classes. The University of Paris thus gained legal personality, becoming one of the earliest universities in Europe. It was organized into four faculties: Arts, Law, Medicine, and Theology, the last being the most famous, attracting scholars from across Europe to lecture and debate.

Genghis Khan personally led about 100,000 Mongol troops in their first major invasion of the Jin. The Jin deployed 450,000 men (claimed 500,000) to defend at Yehuling (present-day Wanquan District, Zhangjiakou, Hebei). The Mongol general Muqali proposed avoiding the main force and sent a cavalry detachment through a mountain path to surprise the Jin command. The Jin commander Wanyan Chengyu fled the battlefield, causing a complete rout; corpses covered the field. The Battle of Yehuling was a turning point in the Mongol-Jin war, as the Jin lost most of their elite forces and could never again mount large-scale field resistance against the Mongols.

v.t: Mongols capture Jin western capital Datong
v.s: After Yehuling, the Mongols pursued their advantage and captured the Jin western capital, Datong (present-day Datong, Shanxi), in September 1211. The Jin commander Hushahu abandoned the city and fled, and the inhabitants suffered mass slaughter. The fall of Datong left the Jin defensive system north of the Yellow River near collapse; cities in Hebei and Shanxi surrendered or fell in quick succession. Emperor Weishao was forced to withdraw most garrisons north of the Yellow River to the central capital (present-day Beijing), becoming strategically passive.

Under pressure from Pope Innocent III, King John of England issued a charter recognizing the University of Oxford as an autonomous academic community and granting it independent jurisdiction from local courts. Over the following centuries, Oxford developed a collegiate system, each college having its own buildings, library, and dining hall, becoming the oldest university in the English-speaking world.

In the spring of 1212, a French shepherd boy named Stephen of Cloyes claimed that Jesus had appeared to him and told him to lead children to the Holy Land and peacefully recover Jerusalem. About 30,000 children and teenagers responded, setting out from Marseille and Cologne toward Italy. However, they had no support from the church or any ruler. Many died of cold and hunger crossing the Alps; the survivors who reached the Italian coast were tricked by ship captains, who took them to North African slave markets. This tragic event reflected the狂热 crusading spirit and the cruel exploitation of ordinary people.

Genghis Khan again led his army south against the Jin, breaking through the Great Wall at Juyong Pass and directly threatening Zhongdu (present-day Beijing). The Jin defenders held out; the Mongols lacked siege equipment, so after months of unsuccessful siege, Genghis Khan dispatched troops to pillage Hebei, Shandong, and Shanxi, capturing almost all prefectures and counties except Zhongdu, Zhengding, and Daming. The Mongols implemented a scorched-earth policy on the North China Plain, destroying vast amounts of farmland and causing immense loss of life, devastating the Jin's northern economic base.

v.t: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
v.s: A Christian coalition led by King Alfonso VIII of Castile, King Peter II of Aragon, and King Sancho VII of Navarre defeated the Almohad Caliph Muhammad al-Nasir at Las Navas de Tolosa (present-day Jaén province, Spain). The Almohad army suffered about 100,000 casualties; the Caliph barely escaped. This battle was the decisive victory of the Reconquista; the Christians gained the upper hand on the Iberian Peninsula, and the Muslims were pushed back to the small Kingdom of Granada.

v.t: Emperor Xuanzong of Jin ascends throne
v.s: Under military pressure from the Mongols and political crisis, Emperor Weishao of Jin was killed by the powerful minister Hushahu. Hushahu then installed Wanyan Xun (grandson of Emperor Shizong) as emperor, known as Emperor Xuanzong. Upon ascending, Xuanzong posthumously demoted Weishao to the rank of Marquis of Donghai and executed Hushahu for treason to appease public anger. However, the Jin situation was hopeless; Xuanzong struggled to maintain his rule under pressure from Mongols, Southern Song, and Western Xia.

Following a suggestion by his generals, Genghis Khan sent an elite cavalry unit through mountain paths bypassing the Jin defenses, suddenly appearing behind the pass. The Jin garrison was taken by surprise, and the defenses quickly collapsed. The fall of Juyong Pass removed the last natural barrier protecting Zhongdu, allowing Mongol cavalry to ride directly to the city walls. Emperor Xuanzong ordered the transfer of grain reserves from Zhongdu to Nanjing (Kaifeng) in preparation for a future move of the capital.

v.t: Francis of Assisi attempts to preach in Morocco
v.s: Burning with a desire for martyrdom, Saint Francis of Assisi set out with 12 friars from Italy, passing through Spain to preach to the Almohad dynasty in Morocco. He hoped to spread Christianity in the Muslim world and possibly achieve martyrdom. However, after reaching Spain he fell seriously ill with malaria and could not continue; he was forced to return to Italy. This failed mission reinforced his commitment to poverty and humility, and he thereafter focused on preaching within Italy.

In 1213, a fire at Canterbury Cathedral destroyed most of the building. The cathedral had previously suffered a fire in 1174 (which led to its Gothic rebuilding). Reconstruction began immediately, employing the most advanced Gothic style, including tall pointed arches and flying buttresses.

v.t: Jin Xuanzong moves capital to Bianjing
v.s: Faced with continued Mongol siege and internal political crisis, Emperor Xuanzong of Jin, over the objections of senior ministers such as Tushan Yi, announced in May 1214 the transfer of the capital from Zhongdu (present-day Beijing) to Bianjing (present-day Kaifeng, Henan). The move used 32,000 carts to transport court valuables and archives, with 70,000 troops escorting the southern journey. Zhongdu fell the following year, and the Jin lost effective control of North China, its territory shrinking sharply to the area south of the Yellow River.

Holy Roman Emperor Otto IV, in alliance with King John of England, the Count of Flanders, and the Count of Boulogne, formed a coalition against France. King Philip II of France met them with inferior forces at Bouvines (in northern France). The French cavalry cleverly outflanked the allied forces in a marshy area, routing the coalition. Otto IV fled in disarray. The battle established French hegemony in Europe; Philip II was honored with the epithet 'Augustus', and the Welfs suffered a devastating blow.

v.t: Albertus Magnus enrolls at the University of Padua
v.s: In 1214 (actually around 1222), Albertus Magnus enrolled at the University of Padua in Italy. Albertus later became one of the most famous scholars of the Middle Ages, proficient in natural sciences, theology, and philosophy. He taught at the University of Paris and was the teacher of Thomas Aquinas.

King John of England, because of his failed wars against France, heavy taxation, and interference in church affairs, provoked widespread baronial discontent. In May 1215, Robert Fitzwalter led a baronial army that occupied London. On 15 June, John was forced to accept 63 articles presented by the barons at Runnymede meadow near Windsor Castle, affixing his seal. The document, known as Magna Carta, included provisions such as: no taxation without common consent of the realm; no free man could be arrested, imprisoned, or dispossessed except by lawful judgment; cities like London had the right to self-government; the church had freedom of elections. Magna Carta established the principles of 'rule of law' and 'the king is below the law', becoming the cornerstone of English constitutional tradition.

v.t: Mongols capture Jin central capital Zhongdu
v.s: After Emperor Xuanzong moved the capital to Bianjing, the Zhongdu garrison commanders Wanyan Cheng-hui and Mo-nian Jin-zhong were unable to defend the city due to a mutiny. On 31 May 1215 the Mongols broke through the outer walls, and the remaining Jin officials committed suicide. The Mongols set fire to the imperial palace, burned the ancestral tablets and books in the imperial temple, and plundered countless treasures, including those of the Liao dynasty. The fall of Zhongdu marked the complete Jin abandonment of North China, making the Mongol Empire the dominant power in East Asia.

Pope Innocent III convened the Fourth Lateran Council at the Lateran Palace, the largest council of the Middle Ages, attended by over 1,200 bishops and abbots. It passed 70 decrees, including: every Catholic must confess to a priest and receive the Eucharist at least once a year; no new religious orders may be established; clergy are forbidden from participating in trial by combat; Jews must wear distinctive clothing to distinguish them from Christians. These decrees profoundly affected Catholic life for centuries.

Pope Innocent III died on 16 July 1216. After a heated conclave, the cardinals elected the elderly Cardinal Cencio Savelli as pope, who took the name Honorius III. Honorius continued the policies of Innocent III, supporting the Franciscans and Dominicans, and in 1217 authorized the launching of the Fifth Crusade.

v.t: French Crown Prince Louis invades England
v.s: English rebel barons invited French Crown Prince Louis (later Louis VIII) to take the English throne. Louis landed at Dover, was warmly welcomed by Londoners, and on 2 June 1216 was proclaimed King of England at St Paul's Cathedral. However, King John died suddenly in October, and his nine-year-old son Henry III succeeded, with William Marshal as regent. Marshal skillfully used diplomacy to win baronial support and eventually defeated Louis, forcing him to sign the Treaty of Lambeth in 1217 and withdraw from England.

v.t: Southern Song prohibits private paper money
v.s: With the rapid expansion of the 'huizi' (paper currency) causing severe inflation, the Southern Song government prohibited private counterfeiting of huizi under penalty of decapitation. It also established a special 'Huizi Bureau' to control printing and issuing, trying to restore the currency's credibility through state monopoly. But the depreciation could not be stopped; people turned to copper coins and silver, and the Song economy fell into chaos in its later period.

v.t: Genghis Khan's western campaign against Khwarazm
v.s: Genghis Khan launched his first major western campaign against the Khwarazmian Empire, using as pretext the massacre of a Mongol trade caravan and the humiliation of Mongol envoys. Leading about 150,000 troops divided into four columns, he employed a strategy of converging attacks, quickly capturing the major cities of Otrar, Bukhara, and others. The Khwarazmshah Muhammad, fearful of the Mongols' strength, abandoned resistance and fled to an island in the Caspian Sea, where he died. This campaign expanded the Mongol Empire into Central Asia and Persia.

Pope Honorius III called for the Fifth Crusade at a council in the Lateran Palace. Unlike previous crusades, the target was the Egyptian port of Damietta; the pope believed that only by destroying Egypt, the economic lifeline of the Ayyubid dynasty, could Jerusalem be recovered. Duke Leopold VI of Austria and King Andrew II of Hungary responded, assembling their armies at Acre.

After the royal army under regent William Marshal won the battles of Lincoln and Sandwich, the French Crown Prince Louis was forced to negotiate with the government of Henry III. On 12 September the Treaty of Lambeth was signed: Louis renounced his claim to the English throne and pledged not to aid English rebels; in return, Henry III paid Louis 10,000 marks in compensation. The English civil war thus ended, and the Plantagenet dynasty continued.

Genghis Khan sent his general Jebe with 20,000 cavalry to conquer the Qara Khitai (Western Liao). The Khitan noble Kuchlug had usurped power and persecuted the Muslim population. Jebe exploited religious tensions by announcing Mongol respect for Islam, gaining support from local Muslims. In 1218 Jebe defeated Kuchlug in the Tarim Basin and executed him. The Qara Khitai, which had existed for nearly a century (1124-1218), was destroyed, and the Mongol Empire's western frontier reached the Amu Darya, directly bordering the Khwarazmian Empire.

In 1229, King James I of Aragon landed on Mallorca and, after months of siege, captured the Muslim-held city of Mallorca. In 1235 Aragon conquered Ibiza. The Balearic Islands became part of the Crown of Aragon and served as an important transit point for Mediterranean trade.

The Mongol western army attacked Khwarazm in four columns. Chagatai and Ögedei besieged Otrar, a strategic city on the Syr Darya. The commander Inalchuq (who had killed the Mongol trade caravan) defended with 50,000 men for five months. The Mongols used counterweight trebuchets day and night, finally breaching the walls in February 1220. Inalchuq was captured and executed by having molten silver poured into his eyes and ears. The brutality of the siege shocked all of Central Asia.

Saint Dominic, founder of the Dominican Order, went to Rome and presented his rule to Pope Honorius III. The rule, based on the Rule of Saint Augustine, emphasized preaching, study, and strict poverty. The pope approved it and formally recognized the Dominicans as a mendicant order. The Dominicans later established chairs at European universities and trained many theological elites.

Genghis Khan personally led the main Mongol army to besiege Samarkand, the new capital of Khwarazm (present-day Samarkand, Uzbekistan). Although the city had a reported 110,000 defenders and 20 war elephants, the Khwarazmshah Muhammad had already fled. The Mongols attacked with counterweight trebuchets and fire pots; the defenders surrendered after only five days. Genghis Khan enslaved 30,000 artisans and strong men and destroyed the outer walls. The fall of Samarkand sealed the fate of the Khwarazmian Empire.

v.t: University of Paris receives papal charter
v.s: Pope Honorius III, at the request of the masters and students of the University of Paris, issued a formal papal charter confirming the university's legal status and guaranteeing its right to make internal regulations, award teaching licenses, and conduct academic judgments. The University of Paris became the center of theological and philosophical studies in Europe, attracting leading scholars such as Thomas Aquinas and Bonaventure.

The Thai chief Pho Khun Sri Indraditya, together with neighboring tribes, declared independence from the Khmer Empire's Angkor dynasty, establishing the Sukhothai Kingdom (meaning 'Dawn of Happiness') with its capital at Sukhothai (present-day Sukhothai province, Thailand). Sri Indraditya adopted a 'paternal' style of rule, emphasizing that the king was the father of the people rather than a god. Sukhothai was the first independent Thai kingdom and is considered the cradle of Thai civilization. Its art and architecture (such as the walking Buddha statues) became symbols of Thai culture.

Genghis Khan ordered the establishment of a relay system (jam) across the Mongol Empire, with a station every 25-30 km providing horses, lodging, and guides. The system's main functions were to transmit military intelligence, official documents, and escort envoys. Special messengers traveled day and night, greatly speeding up communications across the empire. This system was later inherited and improved by the Yuan dynasty and inspired the establishment of modern postal systems.

Khwarazmian prince Jalal al-Din, with the Mongol army approaching, fled to Afghanistan and rallied about 60,000 survivors. He set an ambush at Parwan (north of present-day Kabul, Afghanistan). The Mongol general Shigi Qutuqu pursued with 30,000 cavalry but was defeated due to unfavorable terrain, losing over half his force. This was the worst defeat the Mongols suffered during the western campaign. Upon hearing the news, Genghis Khan led his main army to the Indus River, where he crushed Jalal al-Din, who escaped alone to India.

After the Battle of the Indus, Genghis Khan ordered the massacre of all Khwarazmian cities and the destruction of irrigation systems, turning large areas of Central Asia into desert. The last Khwarazmshah, Muhammad, died during his flight; his son Jalal al-Din fled to India and Persia, eventually killed by local Kurds in 1231. The Khwarazmian Empire (1097-1221), which had existed for only a few decades, was erased from the map and its territories incorporated into the Mongol Empire.

The Fifth Crusade besieged the Egyptian port of Damietta at the Nile mouth for 18 months. Using the tides of the Nile and siege towers, the crusaders finally breached the walls in November 1221. Ayyubid Sultan al-Kamil was forced to abandon the city. However, after occupying Damietta, the crusaders faced logistical problems and the Nile floods; they rejected al-Kamil's offer to exchange Jerusalem for Damietta. In August 1221 they were forced to withdraw, and the crusade ended in failure.

Under pressure from the nobles, King Andrew II of Hungary issued the Golden Bull of 1222. The bull contained 31 articles, including: guaranteeing the land ownership and tax exemption of lesser nobles; requiring the king to convene a diet every year; granting the nobility the right to resist the king by armed force (the 'right of resistance'). The Golden Bull is regarded by Hungarians as their Magna Carta, laying the foundation for noble privileges and constitutional development in Hungary.

v.t: Jebe and Subutai campaign into Rus
v.s: Genghis Khan sent his generals Jebe and Subutai with 25,000 cavalry to conduct a strategic reconnaissance from the Caucasus into the South Russian steppes. The Mongols defeated the Kingdom of Georgia, crossed the Caucasus into the Pontic-Caspian steppe, and defeated the local Kipchaks. The Rus princes, under the leadership of Grand Prince Mstislav of Kiev, assembled a coalition army, but were routed by the Mongols at the Kalka River. This was the first Mongol probing attack on the Rus' lands.

v.t: Southern Song prohibits secret dealings with Mongols
v.s: Having learned that the Jin might ally with the Mongols against the Song and that some merchants along the Yangtze were smuggling goods with the Mongols, the Southern Song court issued a strict decree: anyone corresponding or trading with the Mongols would be executed for treason. The Song shifted its defense focus to the Huai River region and Sichuan, strengthening the Yangtze line in an attempt to maintain a balance among the Song, Jin, and Mongols.

The Mongol forces of Subutai and Jebe met the coalition of the Kievan Rus' and Kipchaks at the Kalka River (present-day Kalmius River, Ukraine). The Rus' coalition had about 80,000 men, the Mongols about 30,000. The Mongols feigned retreat, luring the Rus' cavalry into pursuit, then used horse archers to envelop and annihilate the main Rus' army. Grand Prince Mstislav of Kiev was captured and executed; six other princes and 70 nobles were killed. This battle decimated the forces of the Rus' principalities, clearing the way for the Mongol conquest of Rus' 14 years later.

v.t: First general chapter of the Franciscans
v.s: In 1223, Saint Francis of Assisi held the first general chapter of the Franciscan Order at the Porziuncola near Assisi, attended by about 5,000 friars. The chapter approved the Franciscan Rule (Regula bullata), which was confirmed by Pope Honorius III. It was decided to send friars to preach in Germany, England, Hungary, Morocco, and elsewhere.

v.t: Genghis Khan returns to Mongolia after western campaign
v.s: After six years of western campaigning, Genghis Khan left small garrison forces in the newly conquered Central Asian territories and led his main army eastward back to the Mongolian plateau. On the way, he received news that Western Xia had rebelled and was trying to ally with the Jin against the Mongols. Thus he decided to launch a war against Western Xia immediately upon his return. Genghis Khan's western campaign expanded the Mongol Empire from the Pacific to the Caspian Sea.

v.t: Foundation of the University of Naples
v.s: Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II founded the University of Naples (later named the University of Naples Federico II) in Naples, Italy. It was the first state-run university in Europe, not founded by the church, aimed at training imperial administrative and legal personnel to reduce reliance on the intellectual elites of the Papal States. The university had faculties of law, medicine, and arts, pioneering secular university education in Europe.

The fourth emperor of the Southern Song, Emperor Ningzong Zhao Kuo, died at the Funing Palace in Lin'an at the age of 57. He reigned for 30 years: in the early period Han Tuozhou dominated and launched the failed Kaixi Northern Expedition; in the later period Shi Miyuan was sole chancellor and signed the humiliating Treaty of Jiading. Ningzong had no heir; Chancellor Shi Miyuan and Empress Yang conspired to depose the designated crown prince Zhao Hong and establish Zhao Yun as emperor, who became Emperor Lizong.

Emperor Aizong of Jin, after his accession, tried to revive the military and sent general Yi-La Pu'a north to recover southern Shanxi from Mongol control. The Mongol commander Muqali met him at Jiyuan (present-day Jiyuan, Henan). The Jin army, lacking weapons, horses, and training, was routed by the Mongol cavalry. After this battle, the Jin lost all ability to fight in the field and retreated behind the defenses of Tong Pass and the Yellow River, clinging to existence.

After twenty years, the Albigensian Crusade ended with the incorporation of Languedoc into the royal domain of France. King Louis VIII of France (son of Philip II) personally led a campaign south, capturing the last Cathar strongholds of Avignon and Montségur. The Cathars were massacred or burned at the stake; the survivors went underground. The unique Mediterranean culture of Languedoc was destroyed, and the Kingdom of France extended its power to the Mediterranean coast for the first time.

In December 1237, the Mongol army under Batu captured the important city of Moscow in the Vladimir-Suzdal principality. At that time Moscow was a small border fortress, and its defenders resisted fiercely. After breaching the walls with trebuchets, the Mongols massacred most of the inhabitants and burned the city. The fall of Moscow shocked the Rus' principalities and opened the way for the Mongol establishment of the Golden Horde, which would rule over Russia for more than two centuries.

v.t: First contact between Southern Song and Mongols
v.s: In 1225 (the first year of Baoqing of the Southern Song), Genghis Khan, after finishing his western campaign, sent envoys via Western Xia to the Song border, proposing a joint campaign against the Jin. The Song court was divided: the war party advocated allying with the Mongols to destroy the Jin and avenge the Jingkang humiliation, but some ministers feared 'the lips being gone, the teeth will be cold'. In the end, Emperor Lizong agreed to establish diplomatic relations and exchange envoys with the Mongols. This contact paved the way for the later joint Song-Mongol destruction of the Jin (1234), but also brought the Song face to face with the Mongol military threat.

v.t: Genghis Khan campaigns against Western Xia
v.s: Genghis Khan, citing Western Xia's refusal to send troops to assist the Mongol western campaign and its secret contacts with the Jin, launched a sixth campaign against Western Xia, ignoring his advanced age and failing health. The Mongol army advanced in two columns: one led by Genghis Khan attacked the Hexi region; the other, led by Ögedei, attacked the eastern part. While besieging the Western Xia capital Zhongxing, Genghis Khan was injured in a fall from his horse and, suffering from a high fever, fell gravely ill.

Saint Francis of Assisi, founder of the Franciscan Order, died at the Porziuncola in Assisi at the age of 44. In the last two years of his life, blind and in poor health, he still adhered to poverty and austerity. Two years before his death (1224), while fasting and praying on Mount La Verna, he received the stigmata (the five wounds of Christ). In 1228 Pope Gregory IX canonized him.

In 1245, King Henry III of England ordered the rebuilding of Westminster Abbey in the Gothic style to replace the older Norman church. The new abbey featured tall pointed arches, flying buttresses, and large rose windows. The choir was completed in 1258, but the entire project continued into the late 13th century. Westminster Abbey thereafter became the traditional place for English monarchs to be crowned and buried.

The founder of the Mongol Empire, Genghis Khan (Temüjin), died in his camp at Qingshui County (present-day Qingshui, Gansu) on the western campaign against Western Xia, at the age of 65. On his deathbed, he left three commands: keep his death secret to avoid resistance from Western Xia and enemies; completely destroy Western Xia to leave no future trouble; exploit the enmity between the Song and Jin to ally with the Song against the Jin. His body was secretly transported back to the Mongolian plateau and buried in the Khentii Mountains at a place called Qiyan Valley, its exact location becoming an eternal mystery. All those involved in the funeral procession were executed to preserve secrecy.

After the death of Genghis Khan, the Mongols strictly followed his command and launched a general assault on the Western Xia capital Zhongxing (present-day Yinchuan, Ningxia). The last emperor of Western Xia, Li Xian, surrendered after the city ran out of provisions and relief, and was immediately executed by the Mongols. The Mongols massacred the entire Western Xia royal family and burned the palaces, tombs, and archives. The Western Xia dynasty (1038-1227), which had lasted 189 years, was destroyed, and its unique script became a dead script.

After Genghis Khan's death, following Mongol tradition, his youngest son Tolui served as 'regent', temporarily governing state affairs until a kurultai could elect a new khan. Tolui adhered to his father's wishes and did not claim the throne for himself, instead maintaining stable administration for two years and summoning Genghis Khan's sons and princes to a kurultai to elect the next khan.

v.t: Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II launches Sixth Crusade
v.s: In 1227, Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II formally launched the Sixth Crusade. He was excommunicated by Pope Gregory IX after falling ill and returning home. In 1228 he nevertheless set out for the Holy Land. Through diplomatic negotiations rather than military action, he recovered Jerusalem, Bethlehem, and Nazareth from the Egyptian sultan.

Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II, despite being under excommunication by Pope Gregory IX, led the Sixth Crusade eastward. Instead of fighting, he engaged in diplomatic negotiations with the Ayyubid Sultan al-Kamil, culminating in the Treaty of Jaffa: the Muslims returned Jerusalem, Bethlehem, and Nazareth to Christian control, but retained the Dome of the Rock and the Al-Aqsa Mosque on the Temple Mount, and Christians were not allowed to refortify Jerusalem. In March 1229, Frederick II entered Jerusalem and crowned himself, achieving what had been planned since 1204.

v.t: Ögedei elected Great Khan of the Mongols
v.s: After more than two years of political maneuvering, the Mongol kurultai convened on the banks of the Kherlen River and formally elected Genghis Khan's third son Ögedei as the second Great Khan of the Mongol Empire. At his inauguration, Ögedei declared his intention to continue Genghis's expansionist policies, planning to destroy the Jin and launch a western campaign. Tolui voluntarily stepped down as regent; in gratitude, Ögedei gave Tolui some troops and territories, but the later conflict between Tolui's sons (Möngke and Kublai) and Ögedei's descendants grew into persistent strife.

In the 1230s, at the request of Duke Conrad I of Masovia, the Teutonic Knights began military conquests in Prussia (present-day northeastern Poland and Kaliningrad). The Order built castles such as Thorn and Marienburg on the Vistula. In 1249 the Prussians were forced to accept the Treaty of Christburg and convert to Christianity, but resistance continued until the 1280s. The Teutonic Knights established an independent monastic state.

Frederick II and the Ayyubid sultan al-Kamil signed the Treaty of Jaffa, which stipulated: the Muslims would return Jerusalem, Bethlehem, Nazareth, and the corridor to the Mediterranean to the Crusader states; the Christians would not rebuild the walls of Jerusalem; the two sides would observe a ten-year truce. This diplomatic achievement was strongly condemned by Pope Gregory IX, who considered negotiations with infidels a shameful betrayal.

After his election, Ögedei immediately launched a final offensive against the Jin. The Mongol general Subutai attacked the Jin capital Nanjing (Kaifeng, Henan). Emperor Aizong of Jin mobilized the entire city's population to resist, using 'thunder-crash bombs' (iron-shell bombs) and 'fire-lances' (flamethrowers) to inflict heavy casualties on the Mongols. The Mongols besieged the city for months without success, and due to an outbreak of plague in their ranks, they were forced to lift the siege. However, the Jin were also exhausted and starved, their national power depleted.

Pope Gregory IX approved the founding of the University of Toulouse to promote orthodox doctrine in the Languedoc region where Albigensian heresy had been rampant. The university had faculties of theology, canon law, and arts, primarily training missionaries and judges. It was the oldest university in southern France and played an important role in suppressing Catharism.

v.t: Ögedei devises plan to destroy Jin
v.s: Ögedei convened a military council at Karakorum and drew up the final plan to destroy the Jin: a three-pronged attack, with the central army led by Ögedei himself against Kaifeng, the left army under Öchigin against Shandong, and the right army under Tolui passing through the Southern Song's Hanzhong region to fall on the Jin rear. Tolui sent envoys to the Song requesting passage and a joint attack on the Jin. The Song chancellor Shi Miyuan, hoping to 'avenge Jingkang' by allying with the Mongols against the Jin, agreed to Tolui's request.

v.t: Teutonic Knights establish vassalage over Prussia
v.s: The Grand Master of the Teutonic Order, Hermann von Salza, concluded a treaty with Prussian tribal leaders, under which the Prussians nominally submitted to the Order and provided labor and tribute. This marked the beginning of the Order's colonial rule in Prussia. Over the following decades, through conquest and immigration, the Order thoroughly Germanized Prussia.

Around the 1260s, the Persian Sufi poet Jalal al-Din Rumi began his masterpiece, the Masnavi. This six-volume poem of about 50,000 lines uses allegorical tales to expound Sufi mystical teachings. The Masnavi is known as the 'Qur'an in Persian' and is the highest achievement of Islamic mystical literature. Rumi composed it in Konya, under the influence of his spiritual mentor Shams-i Tabrizi.

v.t: Tolui passes through Song territory to attack Jin
v.s: The Mongol right-wing commander Tolui led 30,000 cavalry from Baoji through Song Hanzhong, following the Han River eastward, bypassing the heavily defended Tong Pass and Yellow River line. Emperor Aizong of Jin was alarmed and rushed the main Jin forces under Wanyan Heda and Yi-La Pu'a (150,000 men) from Dengzhou (present-day Dengzhou, Henan) northward to intercept, intending to trap the Mongols in the Junzhou (present-day Yuzhou, Henan) area.

Ögedei sent the general Sartaq to invade the Goryeo kingdom, on the pretext that Goryeo had killed Mongol envoys. The Mongols captured the northern border cities of Uiju and Guju, and King Gojong of Goryeo was forced to move the capital to Ganghwa Island. The Mongols launched six further large-scale invasions, and Goryeo became a vassal of the Mongols until a peace treaty was signed in 1259.

In 1231, Pope Gregory IX issued the bull 'Excommunicamus', formally establishing the papal Inquisition and authorizing Dominican friars to try heresy cases. Previously, heresy trials were conducted by local bishops with inconsistent procedures. The Inquisition introduced standardized interrogations; defendants had the right to defend themselves but were not allowed to know the names of informants. Although the average number of executions per year was not high, the Inquisition gained a notorious reputation in later centuries for its secret proceedings and use of torture.

The Mongol cavalry under Tolui encountered the main Jin army at Sanfeng Mountain near Junzhou (present-day Yuzhou, Henan). Heavy snow fell; the Jin troops, lacking food and clothing, were freezing and starving, and their morale collapsed. The Mongols took advantage and surrounded the Jin army on three sides, attacking with mounted archers for three days and nights. The Jin commanders Wanyan Heda and Yi-La Pu'a were captured, and the entire 150,000-man elite Jin army was annihilated. After this battle, the Jin no longer possessed any effective field forces, and the dynasty's fall was inevitable.

After Sanfeng Mountain, Ögedei and Tolui joined forces and laid siege to the Jin capital, Nanjing (Kaifeng). Emperor Aizong refused to surrender and ordered the city's defenders to fight to the death. The Jin used 'thunder-crash bombs' and 'fire-lances' to inflict heavy casualties on the Mongols, and dropped 'wooden beams' from the walls to smash Mongol siege engines. The Mongols besieged the city for months without success; inside, food ran out, and cannibalism occurred.

v.t: Death of Saleman bin Abd al-Malik
v.s: Around 1232, Saleman bin Abd al-Malik of the Almohad dynasty died. The Almohads had ruled North Africa and the Iberian Peninsula, but after their crushing defeat at Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212, they were greatly weakened. The dynasty was replaced by the Marinids in the mid-13th century.

After nearly a year of siege, with the city starving, Emperor Aizong of Jin, following his ministers' advice, broke out with a small bodyguard and fled first to Guide (present-day Shangqiu, Henan) and then to Caizhou (present-day Runan, Henan). The Jin general Cui Li staged a coup, killed the remaining officials, and opened the city to the Mongols. After Kaifeng fell, Cui Li sent the Jin empress, imperial concubines, and over 500 members of the imperial clan to the Mongol camp; the Jin dynasty existed in name only.

v.t: Pope Gregory IX establishes the Inquisition
v.s: Pope Gregory IX issued the bull 'Excommunicamus', formally authorizing the Dominican Order to establish inquisitions throughout Europe to try heresy cases. The pope appointed Dominican friars as inquisitors, granting them full authority to gather evidence, interrogate suspects, and pronounce sentences. Inquisitions were subsequently established in France, Italy, Germany, and elsewhere, using torture to deal with heretics, becoming dreaded institutions of the late Middle Ages.

In 1233, the army of the Almohad Caliphate clashed with a coalition led by the Kingdom of Castile near Salé in southern Spain. After the catastrophic defeat at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212), the Almohads were severely weakened and torn by internal rivalries. The defeat at Salé marked the complete collapse of Almohad rule on the Iberian Peninsula, and the Christian kingdoms began the full Reconquista. Castile captured Seville in 1248, and the Almohad remnants in North Africa rapidly disintegrated.

Mongol and Song forces surrounded the last Jin holdout, Caizhou (present-day Runan, Henan). On the night of the ninth day of the first month, seeing that all was lost and unwilling to be a captive sovereign, Emperor Aizong passed the throne to his general Wanyan Chenglin (the Later Emperor Mo). The next morning, the Song-Mongol coalition breached Caizhou. Emperor Aizong hanged himself at Youlan Pavilion; Wanyan Chenglin was also killed in street fighting. The Jin dynasty (1115-1234), which had lasted 120 years, was extinguished. The Song general Meng Gong split the body of Emperor Aizong in two, half for the Song and half for the Mongols.

v.t: Southern Song's Duanping entry into Luoyang
v.s: After the fall of the Jin, the former Song chancellor Shi Miyuan had died, and Emperor Lizong assumed personal rule. He decided to take advantage of the Mongol main force's withdrawal north to recover the old Song capitals: Kaifeng (Eastern Capital), Luoyang (Western Capital), and Yingtian (present-day Shangqiu, Henan). In June, Song generals Zhao Kui and Quan Zicai led their troops north, entering Kaifeng and Luoyang almost without resistance. However, the Mongols quickly returned and heavily defeated the Song army at Luoyang; the Song forces, short of supplies, were forced to retreat south with heavy losses. The failure of the Duanping incursion ruptured Song-Mongol relations, and the Mongols used it as a pretext to launch war against the Southern Song.

On 27 May 1234, King Louis IX of France (Saint Louis) married Margaret of Provence at Sens Cathedral. Margaret was the eldest daughter of Raymond Berengar IV, Count of Provence. The marriage strengthened the ties between France and Provence and paved the way for the eventual French annexation of Provence (1481). Louis IX was known for his piety and just rule, and was canonized as a saint.

Great Khan Ögedei decided to launch a second western campaign, with armies commanded by the eldest sons of the imperial clan, hence called the 'Princes' Western Campaign'. The supreme commander was Batu, son of Jochi; his deputy was Subutai. Other participants included Güyük (son of Ögedei), Möngke (son of Tolui), and Büri (grandson of Chagatai). The army of about 120,000 set out from the Mongolian plateau to conquer the Kipchak steppes and the Rus' principalities, ultimately leading to the establishment of the Golden Horde.

Ögedei, using the Song's 'Duanping entry into Luoyang' as a pretext for breaking their alliance, launched a three-pronged invasion of the Song: the western army under Köden attacked Sichuan; the central army under Ögedei's third son Köchü attacked Xiangyang; the eastern army under Töchü attacked Jianghuai. The Song-Mongol war, lasting 45 years (1235-1279), thus broke out. The Song, under commanders such as Meng Gong and Yu Jie, resisted tenaciously, and the Mongols suffered many setbacks.

In 1235, the Kingdom of Sweden and the Novgorod Republic clashed over control of the Gulf of Finland and the eastern Baltic coast. Swedish forces fought Alexander Nevsky, Prince of Novgorod, near the Neva River. In 1240 Alexander decisively defeated the Swedes in the Battle of the Neva, earning the epithet 'Nevsky'. The struggle for Karelia and Finland continued for centuries.

v.t: Mali Empire established: Mansa Musa's golden age
v.s: In 1235, the Mandinka leader Sundiata Keita defeated King Sumanguru of Sosso at the Battle of Kirina and established the Mali Empire. Mali controlled the West African gold and salt trades, with its capital at Niani becoming a center of learning. The empire reached its zenith under Mansa Musa (1312-1337); during his pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324 he distributed so much gold in Cairo that the Egyptian gold price dropped for a decade. The Sankore University in Timbuktu became one of the world's most famous Islamic centers of learning.

Batu's western expedition army reached the Volga River basin and captured Bulgar, the capital of the Bulgars (present-day Republic of Tatarstan, Russia). The Mongols destroyed the city's palaces, mosques, and libraries, and massacred a large part of the population. Volga Bulgaria, a state that had existed for centuries (c. 660-1236), was destroyed, and its territory incorporated into the Golden Horde.

The central Mongol army under Ögedei's third son Köchü captured the strategic Southern Song city of Xiangyang (present-day Xiangyang, Hubei). Xiangyang was the gateway to the middle Yangtze River; the Song garrison, commanded by Zhao Fan, was thrown into disarray by internal conflict, and the Mongols took the opportunity to breach the city. The fall of Xiangyang opened a huge gap in the Song's Yangtze defense line, allowing the Mongols to advance deep into the middle Yangtze plain. Although later recovered by Meng Gong, Xiangyang's strategic position was compromised.

Pope Gregory IX died in Rome on 22 August 1241 at about age 101. During his pontificate (1227-1241), he struggled fiercely with Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II, excommunicating him twice. Gregory established the papal Inquisition (1231), authorizing the Dominicans to try heresy. He also actively promoted crusading, organizing the Sixth Crusade. Gregory IX was one of the most powerful popes of the 13th century.

v.t: Mongols conquer the Grand Duchy of Vladimir
v.s: Batu's Mongol army crossed the frozen Volga in winter and attacked the Grand Duchy of Vladimir. The Mongols captured the cities of Ryazan, Moscow, Vladimir, and more than a dozen others. Grand Prince Yuri II of Vladimir was killed in the Battle of the Sit River. In their sieges, the Mongols made extensive use of counterweight trebuchets and gunpowder weapons introduced from China; the wooden fortresses of the Rus' were no match for heavy trebuchets.

v.t: First Mongol invasion of Goryeo ends
v.s: Since the 1231 invasion, the Mongols had ravaged Goryeo, forcing the court to move to Ganghwa Island. King Gojong sent envoys to the Mongols to sue for peace, expressing submission. Ögedei accepted Goryeo's surrender and withdrew most of his troops, but left 72 darughachis (overseers) to supervise Goryeo governance. Goryeo became a vassal of the Mongol Empire but retained a degree of semi-independence.

In 1237, Batu's Mongol army captured Kyiv, marking the climax of the second Mongol western campaign. The Mongols used large trebuchets and counterweight catapults in the siege; after the walls were breached in several places, the defenders fought fierce street battles. The Mongols massacred the population and burned buildings including St. Sophia Cathedral. Kyiv, the 'mother of Rus' cities', was completely destroyed. The Mongols then established the Golden Horde (Kipchak Khanate), which ruled the Rus' principalities for more than two centuries.

Batu's western expedition army captured Kiev, the capital of Kievan Rus'. The Mongols used counterweight trebuchets to collapse the walls; the city's defenders fought desperately. After the city fell, the Mongols slaughtered and plundered, killing the metropolitan and most of the inhabitants. The once-glorious capital of the Rus' was almost razed to the ground and never recovered. The Mongol domination of Rus' (the 'Tatar yoke') formally began, lasting 240 years.

v.t: Song general Meng Gong recovers Xiangyang
v.s: After training troops in Luzhou (present-day Luzhou, Sichuan), the famous Song anti-Mongol general Meng Gong marched north to counterattack Xiangyang. Taking advantage of the withdrawal of the main Mongol force northward, he used a combined land and water assault to recapture Xiangyang. Meng Gong then rebuilt the city walls, strengthened fortifications, and placed iron chains across the Han River to block the Mongol navy. Xiangyang subsequently held out for over thirty years, falling again only in 1273.

In 1238, Batu's Mongol army invaded the Kingdom of Poland. After fierce resistance by the Silesian coalition led by Henry II, the Mongols annihilated the Polish-German army at the Battle of Legnica on 9 April 1241. Duke Henry II of Silesia was killed. The Mongols then plundered widely across Poland, burning cities including Krakow. However, needing to rejoin the main force to attack Hungary, they did not occupy Poland permanently.

A Mongol contingent under Chormaqan advanced westward from Persia and reached the Syrian border controlled by the Ayyubid dynasty. The Mongols captured the important Mesopotamian city of Amida (present-day Diyarbakır, Turkey) and then entered northern Syria, plundering the outskirts of Aleppo. The Ayyubid sultan al-Kamil was forced to pay tribute and sue for peace.

v.t: Statute of Merton enacted in England
v.s: Under pressure from his barons, King Henry III of England issued the Statute of Merton, allowing lords to enclose wasteland for pasture and restricting the rights of peasants to graze on common land. This marked the early beginning of the English enclosure movement; many yeomen lost their land and were forced to become farm laborers.

v.t: Thomas of Savoy becomes regent of Jerusalem
v.s: In 1239, Count Thomas I of Savoy became regent of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, one of the crusader states in the Levant. Thomas tried to coordinate the various crusader factions against the rising Mamluk threat from Egypt. However, internal divisions among the crusaders prevented effective resistance.

Batu divided his forces to invade Eastern Europe: Baidar and Kadan led an army into Poland to cover the main force's attack on Hungary. The Mongols burned the Polish cities of Sandomierz and Krakow, and near Legnica in Silesia encountered a Polish-Teutonic coalition led by Duke Henry II of Silesia.

The West African king Sundiata Keita of the Mali Empire defeated King Sumanguru of the Sosso kingdom at the Battle of Kirina, unified the Mandinka tribes, and established the Mali Empire. Sundiata then made Niani (in present-day Guinea) his capital and proclaimed the Mandinka Constitution (the Kouroukan Fouga). The Mali Empire soon replaced the Ghana Empire as the most powerful state in West Africa, famous for its gold wealth.

Pope Gregory IX issued a bull granting universities such as Paris, Oxford, and Bologna the authority to award bachelor's, master's, and doctoral degrees. The right to award degrees marked the maturation of the medieval university system; graduates could teach in any Christian country without further examination by the local church.

The Majapahit Kingdom was established by Raden Wijaya in 1293 on the eastern part of Java. Taking advantage of the Yuan dynasty's expedition to Java, Raden Wijaya used Yuan forces to defeat the Singhasari kingdom, then turned on the Yuan and drove them out, establishing an independent kingdom. Under the leadership of Prime Minister Gajah Mada (1336-1364), Majapahit swore the 'Palapa Oath' and unified the entire Nusantara archipelago, effectively controlling much of present-day Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines. Majapahit was one of the last great Hindu-Buddhist empires in Southeast Asia.

Baidar's Mongol army encountered a Polish-Teutonic coalition near Legnica in Silesia. The Mongols used their feigned retreat tactic, drawing the heavily armored knights into an ambush and then shooting them from both flanks. Duke Henry II of Silesia was killed; the Grand Master of the Teutonic Order was severely wounded. The European coalition was annihilated, but the Mongols voluntarily withdrew upon learning of Ögedei's death, sparing Europe from complete conquest.

The Mongol Great Khan Ögedei, due to heavy drinking, suffered a stroke and died suddenly at a winter hunting ground in Karakorum at age 56. His widow, Töregene Khatun, assumed regency without immediately convening a kurultai to elect a new khan, instead promoting her own supporters and excluding rivals. Ögedei's death caused the Mongol western army to withdraw from Europe, saving Europe from being completely overrun.

Batu's main Mongol army defeated the European coalition led by King Bela IV of Hungary at Mohi (present-day Mohi, Hungary). The Mongols crossed the Tisza River using pontoon bridges and outflanked the Hungarian camp from the rear. King Bela IV escaped, but the entire Hungarian elite cavalry was destroyed. The Mongols then captured and burned Buda and Pest (the cities that later became Budapest).

Prince Alexander Nevsky of Novgorod met the Teutonic Knights on the frozen Lake Peipus. Nevsky had his infantry form a line in the center of the ice to lure the knights into charging; the weight of the knights' armor caused the ice to break, and many knights drowned. The Teutonic Order was forced to sue for peace, abandoning all claims to Rus' territory. This battle is regarded by Russia as a symbol of national spirit, and Nevsky was canonized as a saint.

After the death of Ögedei Khan, Batu withdrew the Mongol western army from Hungary and Poland to the South Russian steppes. Batu established the capital of the Golden Horde at Sarai on the lower Volga (near present-day Astrakhan, Russia). The Golden Horde became one of the four khanates of the Mongol Empire, ruling over Rus' for two and a half centuries.

In 1242, after conquering the Indus Valley, Mongol generals led a force across the Himalayan foothills into Kashmir. The Mongols captured some towns but could not establish long-term control. The ruler of Kashmir purchased peace by paying tribute to the Mongols. Thereafter, Kashmir became a vassal state of the Mongol Empire, regularly sending silk, saffron, and fine horses to the Great Khan. This invasion was part of the Mongol expansion southward, but the mountainous terrain prevented complete conquest.

Sultan Kaykhusraw II of the Sultanate of Rum (a Seljuk Turkic state in Anatolia) faced a Mongol army under Baiju at Köse Dağ. The Mongols used their standard light cavalry tactics, destroying the Seljuk heavy cavalry with archery. The defeated Sultanate of Rum became a vassal of the Mongol Ilkhanate, and Seljuk power declined.

After an 18-month conclave, the cardinals elected Cardinal Sinibaldo Fieschi of Genoa as pope, who took the name Innocent IV. Immediately after his accession, Innocent IV came into sharp conflict with Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II, excommunicating him and calling on European rulers to depose Frederick.

In 1243, the Mongol general Baiju Noyan invaded Anatolia, defeated the Seljuk army of the Sultanate of Rum at the Battle of Köse Dağ, and then captured the city of Erzurum. The Mongols burned the city and massacred the inhabitants. The fall of Erzurum forced the Sultanate of Rum to submit and pay tribute to the Mongols. Eastern and central Anatolia thus came under the control of the Ilkhanate.

Khwarazmian remnant horsemen, invited by the Ayyubid sultan al-Salih, marched south and attacked Jerusalem. On 15 July 1244 the Khwarazmians breached the walls, massacred the inhabitants, and burned the Church of the Holy Sepulchre and other Christian buildings. Jerusalem, which Christians had regained by treaty in 1229, was lost again, and would not return to Christian hands until 1917.

After the fall of Jerusalem, the crusader states allied with anti-Ayyubid factions and met the Khwarazmians and Egyptian army at La Forbie (in present-day Israel). The coalition was crushed; the armies of the Kingdom of Jerusalem and the Principality of Antioch were annihilated. Christian power in the Levant never recovered.

In 1244, the Mongol Empire invaded Anatolia (Asia Minor) and defeated the army of the Sultanate of Rum near Erzurum. The Seljuk Turks, rulers of the Sultanate of Rum, were forced to submit and pay tribute to the Mongols, becoming vassals of the Ilkhanate. The Mongols thereafter stationed troops in eastern Anatolia, levied taxes, and appointed governors. This invasion profoundly altered the political landscape of Anatolia, weakening the Seljuks and creating conditions for the later rise of the Ottoman Empire.

Pope Innocent IV, fleeing from the military threat of Emperor Frederick II, took refuge in Lyon, France, where he convened the 13th ecumenical council, the First Council of Lyon. The council proclaimed the excommunication of Frederick II and called on European rulers to depose him. It also called for a Seventh Crusade, but received little positive response.

v.t: Flourishing of Southern Song printing at Jianyang
v.s: The printing workshops at Masha and Chonghua towns in Jianyang (present-day Nanping, Fujian) reached their peak. A total of 37 workshops were recorded, producing more than half of the nation's books. The Jianyang workshops specialized in popular literature, examination guides, and medical books, using cheap bamboo paper and woodblock printing, which greatly reduced book costs and made them affordable to ordinary scholars. This publishing center promoted the spread of Song culture and education.

In 1245, Mongol generals led an invasion of northern India, attempting to conquer the Delhi Sultanate. The Mongols crossed the Indus and captured the area around Lahore. The Delhi Sultan Balban mobilized a large army to meet them. The Mongols fought the Indian war elephants on the plains north of Delhi but, unaccustomed to the heat of South Asia, withdrew. The Mongols invaded India several times thereafter (up to 1306) but never managed to break through the Delhi Sultanate's defenses into the interior.

After five years of regency by Töregene and political maneuvering, the Mongol kurultai finally convened at Karakorum and elected Güyük, the eldest son of Ögedei, as the new Great Khan. Upon accession, Güyük purged the corrupt officials of Töregene's faction, executing Fatima (who had sold offices during the regency) and demoting Töregene's supporters. He also strengthened central authority and limited the power of princes and generals.

v.t: Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II dies
v.s: Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor, died at Castel Fiorentino in southern Italy at the age of 52. Frederick was one of the most remarkable rulers of the Middle Ages, fluent in six languages, founder of the University of Naples, and leader of the Sixth Crusade which recovered Jerusalem through negotiation. His death marked the decline of the Hohenstaufen dynasty, plunging Germany into the 'Great Interregnum'.

v.t: First diplomatic contact between Mongol Empire and Papacy
v.s: In 1246, Pope Innocent IV sent the Franciscan friar John of Plano Carpini to the Mongol capital Karakorum. Carpini attended the enthronement of Güyük Khan and brought back a letter from Güyük demanding the submission of Western rulers. This was the first official diplomatic contact between the Mongol Empire and the Roman Papacy.

The Song mathematician Qin Jiushao completed his magnum opus 'Shushu Jiuzhang' (Mathematical Treatise in Nine Sections) in 18 volumes after ten years of work. The treatise systematically discussed numerical solutions of higher-order equations (the zheng-fu kai fang method), the Dayan qiu yi method (solving simultaneous linear congruences), and applied problems in surveying, taxation, and military affairs. The Dayan qiu yi method predates Gauss's work on congruences by over 500 years, and the zheng-fu kai fang method predates Ruffini-Horner by more than 500 years. 'Shushu Jiuzhang' marked a new peak in ancient Chinese mathematics.

Köden, second son of Ögedei, sent general Dorta with an army into Tibet, subduing tribal chiefs and Buddhist monasteries. The Sakya leader Sakya Pandita was invited to Liangzhou (present-day Wuwei, Gansu) to negotiate with Köden. Sakya Pandita reached an agreement on behalf of the Tibetan factions: Tibet would submit to the Mongols, and the Sakya order would administer Tibetan religious and political affairs. Tibet was formally incorporated into the Mongol Empire.

v.t: Great Interregnum in Holy Roman Empire begins
v.s: After Frederick II's death, Conrad IV succeeded but soon died, and the Hohenstaufen male line became extinct. The German princes fought for the throne, and the Holy Roman Empire entered a 19-year 'Great Interregnum' (1254-1273) during which there was no universally recognized emperor, and central authority was virtually nonexistent.

Güyük Khan, while marching west against Batu, suddenly died of illness (aggravated by heavy drinking and poor health) in Qinghe County, Xinjiang, at age 42. His widow, Oghul Qaimish, became regent but lacked political ability, so the Mongol Empire again fell into succession chaos. Batu and the Tolui princes began plotting to install Möngke as Great Khan.

v.t: King Louis IX of France launches Seventh Crusade
v.s: The pious King Louis IX (Saint Louis), on hearing that Jerusalem had fallen again, vowed to launch a crusade to recover the Holy Land. He led 35,000 troops from France, assembled at Cyprus, and planned to attack Damietta in Egypt. This was the last major crusade led by a European monarch.

In 1264, the English chancellor Walter de Merton founded Merton College, Oxford. Merton established a college statute, making Merton one of the earliest Oxford colleges to adopt a residential and self-governing model. Merton College became known for mathematics and astronomy; the 14th-century Merton School made important contributions to kinematics, paving the way for Galileo's later work.

The Seventh Crusade, under Louis IX, sailed from Cyprus and attacked Egypt. The crusaders easily captured the Nile port of Damietta; the Muslim defenders abandoned the city without much resistance. However, Louis IX did not immediately advance on Cairo but waited for reinforcements led by his brother Alphonse, losing the initiative.

v.t: Lübeck becomes leader of the Hanseatic League
v.s: The North German city of Lübeck concluded a treaty with Hamburg to protect trade routes between the Baltic and the North Sea. This treaty is seen as the origin of the Hanseatic League. Lübeck gradually became the league's leading city, uniting over 200 cities around the Baltic and dominating northern European trade for 300 years.

In 1249, the Mongol general Yisuge (Yisuge/Iljigidei) led an army into northern Syria, capturing Aleppo. The Mongol advance caused panic among the crusader states and Arab powers. However, Yisuge was executed during the political purges after Möngke's accession in 1251.

When the Ayyubid sultan al-Salih died, his favorite concubine Shajar al-Durr concealed his death and, with the support of Mamluk (slave-soldier) generals Aybak and Baibars, defeated al-Salih's son Turanshah and seized power. Shajar al-Durr became a rare female ruler in the Islamic world, but she soon ceded power to Aybak. Aybak established the Mamluk Sultanate (1250-1517), and Egypt entered the Mamluk era.

v.t: Thomas Aquinas enters the University of Paris
v.s: The Italian theologian Thomas Aquinas entered the University of Paris to study under the famous philosopher Albertus Magnus. At Paris, Aquinas devoted himself to the study of Aristotle's works, attempting to integrate Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology. His masterwork, the 'Summa Theologica', later became the official philosophy of the Catholic Church, and Aquinas was declared a Doctor of the Church and the Angelic Doctor.

A group of Polynesian navigators, using double-hulled canoes and guided by stars and ocean currents, sailed from the Marquesas or Tahiti on a journey of over 4,000 km, successfully reaching the Hawaiian Islands. There they established tribal societies based on a chiefdom system and developed a distinct Hawaiian culture, including hula dancing and surfing. This is one of the greatest feats of human navigation.

The Maya city-state of Mayapan in the Yucatán Peninsula, under the leadership of the Cocom dynasty, united with Uxmal and Chichen Itza to form the Mayapan League. Through marriage and political alliances, the Cocom brought most of the peninsula's city-states into the league, ending the long-standing civil wars of the Classic Maya period. Mayapan became the political center of Postclassic Maya civilization.

Batu convened a kurultai on the banks of the Alataw River, which elected Möngke, the eldest son of Tolui, as the fourth Great Khan of the Mongol Empire. Princes of the Ögedei and Chagatai lineages refused to recognize him, but Möngke, backed by Batu's strong military support, forcibly assumed the throne. He then launched a great purge, executing dozens of princes and generals from the Ögedei and Chagatai lines, consolidating his power.

After decades of civil war in Norway (1130-1240), the throne finally passed to Haakon IV, son of Skule Bårdsson. Haakon IV pursued centralization, reduced noble power, and issued a unified legal code, ushering in a golden age for medieval Norway.

In 1252, Great Khan Möngke ordered his brother Kublai to lead 100,000 troops on an expedition against the Dali kingdom (present-day Yunnan). Kublai divided his forces into three columns, crossed the Sichuan plateau and the Jinsha River, and captured Dali city in 1253. King Duan Xingzhi surrendered. Following Kublai's advice, Möngke allowed the Duan royal family to rule Dali hereditarily but stationed darughachis (overseers) there. The conquest of Dali gave the Mongols a strategic route to attack the Southern Song from the southwest and, for the first time, brought Yunnan under central government control.

Great Khan Möngke sent his brother Hulagu to lead a western campaign, aiming to conquer western Persia and the Abbasid Caliphate in Mesopotamia. Hulagu led 50,000 elite Mongol troops (including more than a thousand Han artillery units) and was supported by Armenian and Georgian vassal forces. The campaign ultimately led to the fall of Baghdad and the end of the Abbasid Caliphate.

Pope Innocent IV died in Naples at about age 70. During his reign, he waged a long struggle against Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II, successfully weakening the Hohenstaufen dynasty. He also convened the First Council of Lyon and called for a crusade.

In September 1296, the Republic of Florence laid the foundation for the Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore. The architect Arnolfo di Cambio designed the monumental Gothic facade. The cathedral's dome (built by Brunelleschi, 1420-1436) is a milestone of Renaissance architecture, measuring 42 meters in diameter.

Möngke sent his brother Kublai on an expedition to conquer the Dali kingdom (present-day Yunnan). Kublai used an outflanking strategy, marching from Lintao, Gansu, through Sichuan and eastern Tibet, covering over 3,000 li, and captured Dali city (present-day Dali, Yunnan).
