The last Dali emperor, Duan Xingzhi, was captured, and the Dali kingdom (937-1253), which had lasted more than 300 years, was destroyed. Yunnan came under direct central government control for the first time.

The Venetian merchants Niccolò and Maffeo Polo (father and uncle of Marco Polo) set out from Constantinople to trade in the Golden Horde. They later reached Bukhara in Central Asia, where they met envoys from Hulagu to the Yuan court, and decided to continue eastward to China. This journey made the Polo brothers among the first European merchants to reach China.

In 1253, the English nobleman John de Balliol founded Balliol College at Oxford. It is one of the three oldest colleges at Oxford (after University College and Merton College). Balliol College was known for theology and law in the Middle Ages.

The English Franciscan friar Roger Bacon entered Oxford University and later became one of Europe's most learned scholars. He advocated studying natural phenomena through experiment and mathematics, criticizing the empty debates of scholastic philosophy. Bacon made important contributions to optics, chemistry, and astronomy, and was called the 'Admirable Doctor'.

v.t: Great Interregnum in Holy Roman Empire
v.s: After Conrad IV's death, the empire had been without an emperor for four years. The German princes split into two factions: one supporting Count William II of Holland, the other supporting King Alfonso X of Castile. The empire was in a state of anarchy; the princes acted independently, and feudal fragmentation increased.

In 1254, the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt and the Mongol Ilkhanate clashed along the Syrian border. The Mamluks successfully fended off small Mongol incursions. The struggle for Syria continued for decades, culminating in the Mamluk victory at Ain Jalut in 1260.

The two Italian maritime republics, Venice and Genoa, went to war over dominance of trade in the eastern Mediterranean. Venice allied with the Empire of Nicaea, obtaining trade privileges in Constantinople; Genoa allied with the Latin Empire. The fighting was concentrated in the Aegean and Black Seas, with heavy losses on both sides.

The Mongol general Uriyangkhadai (son of Subutai) led an army into the territory of the Ly dynasty in Vietnam, aiming to outflank the Southern Song from the south. The Mongols captured the Ly capital Thang Long (present-day Hanoi), and the Ly emperor fled to an island. However, due to climate and the Vietnamese guerrilla tactics, the Mongols withdrew. The Ly dynasty paid tribute to the Mongols, and the Mongols withdrew.

v.t: King Afonso III of Portugal conquers Algarve
v.s: In 1255 (actually 1249-1250), King Afonso III of Portugal completed the conquest of the Algarve, capturing the last Moorish stronghold of Faro. This marked the completion of the Portuguese Reconquista; Portugal's territorial borders were largely set.

Hulagu's Mongol army besieged Alamut Castle in northern Iran, the headquarters of the Nizari Ismailis (the Assassins, known as the 'Old Man of the Mountain'). The Assassins had repeatedly murdered Mongol generals and Islamic rulers. The Mongols used counterweight trebuchets for months; the last Assassin imam, Rukn ad-Din Khurshah, surrendered. Hulagu then had all the Assassins executed and the castle completely destroyed. This feared assassination organization was wiped out.

v.t: Kublai establishes his court at Jinlianchuan
v.s: Kublai established a court at Jinlianchuan (present-day Guyuan, Hebei) in the southern part of the Mongolian steppe, recruiting many Han Chinese Confucian scholars and advisers such as Liu Bingzhong, Yao Shu, and Xu Heng. Under their influence, Kublai gradually adopted Chinese ways, using Han methods to rule the Central Plain, promoting land colonization, water conservancy, and schools. This laid the foundation for his later establishment of the Yuan dynasty, but also aroused suspicion from Möngke Khan and other Mongol nobles.

Dante Alighieri was born in 1265 in Florence, Italy, into a minor noble family. Dante is one of Italy's greatest poets, known as the 'father of the Italian language'. He wrote the epic poem 'Divine Comedy', which describes his journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise, reflecting the religious, philosophical, and political currents of the late Middle Ages. Dante's work marks the transition from medieval to Renaissance literature and has profoundly influenced Western literature.

Hulagu's Mongol army besieged Baghdad, the capital of the Abbasid Caliphate. The city had about 50,000 defenders, and Caliph al-Musta'sim refused to surrender, trusting in divine protection. The Mongols used counterweight trebuchets and on 10 February broke through the gates. The Mongols massacred the inhabitants for seven days; an estimated 800,000 people died, and the caliph was executed. The Abbasid Caliphate (750-1258) was destroyed, and the Islamic Golden Age ended.

Students at the University of Paris clashed with local citizens. Regent Blanche of Castile, mother of Louis IX, ordered a crackdown, resulting in several student deaths. The university faculty and students went on strike and threatened to move to Oxford or Cambridge. Pope Alexander IV mediated and eventually issued a charter confirming the university's judicial autonomy.

King Henry III of England granted a royal charter to the University of Cambridge, confirming its legal status and autonomy. Cambridge thus gained academic parity with Oxford, with the right to establish colleges, award degrees, and appoint professors.

After a 12-day siege, Hulagu's Mongol army stormed the Abbasid capital of Baghdad. The Mongols used counterweight trebuchets and incendiaries to destroy part of the wall. On 13 February the Mongols entered the city and began a seven-day massacre. An estimated 800,000 residents were killed; the caliph al-Musta'sim was put into a sack and trampled to death by horses. Baghdad's libraries and the House of Wisdom were burned, and countless books were thrown into the Tigris, turning the river black with ink. The 500-year rule of the Abbasid dynasty came to an end.

Möngke Khan led the main Mongol army of over 40,000 to besiege the Southern Song fortress of Diaoyucheng (present-day Hechuan, Chongqing). The fortress was strategically located on a steep hill, and the Song commander Wang Jian led a determined defense. The Mongols used siege towers and counterweight trebuchets for five months without success, suffering heavy losses. Möngke was hit by a Song artillery stone (or died of plague) and died beneath the fortress in August of the following year. Diaoyucheng thus came to be known as the 'Place where God broke his whip'.

English barons, led by Simon de Montfort, forced King Henry III to accept the Provisions of Oxford. The provisions established a permanent council of 15 barons, without whose consent the king could not appoint officials, declare war, or levy taxes. Furthermore, parliament was to be convened three times a year. This was an important step in English constitutional development, placing substantial limits on the monarchy.

Möngke Khan was severely wounded (or contracted plague) during the siege of Diaoyucheng and died at the camp below the fortress on 11 August at age 52. His death caused the main Mongol army to withdraw, allowing the Southern Song to survive temporarily. After Möngke's death, his brothers Kublai and Ariq Böke fought for the throne, and the Mongol Empire split. The battle of Diaoyucheng is one of the most influential battles in world history.

v.t: Kublai makes peace with Southern Song
v.s: After Möngke's death, Kublai, wishing to return north to contest the throne, made peace with Song chancellor Jia Sidao. The agreement: the Song would pay the Mongols an annual tribute of 200,000 taels of silver and 200,000 bolts of silk; the Mongols recognized Song control over the region south of the Yangtze River; the two sides would take the Yangtze as the boundary. Upon returning, Jia Sidao falsely reported a great victory to Emperor Lizong, claiming he had repelled the Mongols, concealing the territorial and financial concessions.

v.t: Treaty of Paris between Louis IX and Henry III
v.s: In 1259, King Louis IX of France and King Henry III of England signed the Treaty of Paris. Under its terms, Henry III renounced his claims to Normandy, Anjou, Poitou, and other territories, but retained Gascony (Aquitaine) as a fief of the French crown. Louis IX recognized Henry's rights to some border territories. The treaty ended the Angevin wars between England and France.

Kublai convened a kurultai at his summer capital Kaiping (present-day Duolun, Inner Mongolia) and declared himself Great Khan of the Mongol Empire. His younger brother Ariq Böke was meanwhile proclaimed Great Khan at Karakorum by the princes of the Ögedei and Chagatai lines. The Mongol Empire descended into a four-year civil war (1260-1264). Kublai, relying on resources from China, ultimately defeated Ariq Böke.

The Mamluk sultan Qutuz, with about 20,000 cavalry, met the Mongol vanguard under Kitbuqa (about 12,000 men) at Ain Jalut in Palestine (meaning 'Spring of Goliath'). The Mamluks used an ambush tactic, feigning retreat to draw the Mongols into a valley, then charged with heavy cavalry from three sides. Kitbuqa was killed, and the Mongol army was annihilated. This was the first major defeat of the Mongol western campaign, halting Mongol expansion into Egypt and North Africa.

The Italian poet Dante Alighieri was born in Florence. He was one of the greatest poets of the Middle Ages; his masterwork, the 'Divine Comedy', written in the Italian vernacular, describes a journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise. The 'Divine Comedy' established the Italian national language, and Dante is known as the 'Father of the Italian Language'.

The theology faculty of the University of Paris issued a ban on teaching Aristotle's natural philosophy, fearing it might conflict with Christian doctrine. However, the ban was not strictly enforced; scholars such as Thomas Aquinas continued to study Aristotle and eventually accomplished the synthesis of Christian theology and Aristotelian philosophy.

Emperor Michael VIII Palaiologos of the Empire of Nicaea led his forces to recapture Constantinople, overthrowing the Latin Empire. Michael VIII was crowned Byzantine emperor, the patriarch of Constantinople was restored, and the Palaiologos dynasty began its rule of Byzantium (1261-1453). The last Latin emperor, Baldwin II, fled to western Europe. Although the Byzantine Empire was restored, it had lost much of its former power and territory.

Kublai issued the Zhongtong Yuanbao Jiaochao (Zhongtong paper currency) in the Central Plain, backed by silver and convertible into silver. The Zhongtong note was the first inconvertible (initially convertible) paper currency to circulate across all of China, laying the foundation for the Yuan dynasty's unified economy. Later Yuan emperors continued to issue paper money, but over-issuance led to severe inflation.

v.t: Polo brothers arrive at Yuan Shangdu
v.s: After a three-year journey, Niccolò and Maffeo Polo arrived at Kublai's summer capital Shangdu (present-day Zhenglan Banner, Inner Mongolia). Kublai received them warmly, asked about European customs, and sent them as envoys to the pope. The Polo brothers returned to Europe in 1269, carrying letters from Kublai to the pope.

Due to internal conflicts among its chieftains, the Icelandic Free State decided to accept the protection of King Haakon IV of Norway. Haakon sent envoys to Iceland, and the local chiefs signed an agreement recognizing the Norwegian king's sovereignty and paying taxes. Iceland ended about 300 years of self-rule (930-1262), becoming part of the Kingdom of Norway until full independence in 1944.

The king of Goryeo ceased recognizing Mongol suzerainty, and Kublai sent a second invasion force. The Mongols again captured northern Goryeo cities, and the Goryeo court continued to take refuge on Ganghwa Island. Over the following decades, six invasions were launched; Goryeo eventually submitted under relentless Mongol pressure.

v.t: Flourishing of the Republic of Siena
v.s: In the 13th century, the Republic of Siena reached its peak of economic and cultural prosperity. Siena was one of Europe's most important financial centers; its bankers lent to popes and European monarchs. In 1297 Siena built the magnificent Palazzo Pubblico (Torre del Mangia, 102 m high). The Black Death of 1348 halved Siena's population, and the city declined thereafter.

Kublai defeated the main forces of Ariq Böke at the Battle of Shimutu Nao'er in the northwest, and Ariq Böke retreated to the Altai Mountains. Kublai cut off Ariq Böke's supply lines, causing famine. Ariq Böke's generals surrendered to Kublai, and Ariq Böke was forced to submit to Kublai in 1264. The Mongol civil war ended, and Kublai became the sole Great Khan, but the Chagatai and Ögedei khanates refused to recognize his authority.

Dante began composing the 'Divine Comedy' in Florence (completed in 1320). The poem is divided into three parts: 'Inferno', 'Purgatorio', and 'Paradiso', each with 33 cantos, plus a prologue for a total of 100. Dante used terza rima and wrote in the first person, describing his journey through the three realms guided by Virgil and Beatrice. The 'Divine Comedy' comprehensively reflects the medieval worldview, theology, and philosophy, and is the supreme achievement of Italian literature.

In 1273, the Mamluk sultan Baibars, after a long siege, captured Masyaf Castle, the last major stronghold of the Assassins (Nizaris) in Syria. The Assassins were known for political murder (the English word 'assassin' derives from their name). Baibars systematically destroyed all their fortresses in the Syrian mountains; the survivors fled to India and Persia. The Assassins as an independent political force disappeared.

v.t: Simon de Montfort summons English parliament
v.s: After defeating King Henry III, English baronial leader Simon de Montfort summoned the first English parliament to include knights of the shire and burgesses. This parliament, known as the 'Simon Parliament', included not only nobles and bishops but also two knights from each shire and two burgesses from each borough. It marked the formal establishment of representative government in England and is the origin of the modern parliamentary system.

The Italian theologian Thomas Aquinas began writing his masterwork, the 'Summa Theologica', at the University of Paris. The vast work is divided into three parts: the first on God, the second on ethics, and the third on Christ and the sacraments. Aquinas attempted to systematically present Christian theology using Aristotelian philosophy, resolving the tension between faith and reason.

King Gojong of Goryeo was forced to accept the Mongol terms for withdrawal: the crown prince of Goryeo would go to Mongolia as a hostage; the Goryeo king would personally pay homage to Kublai; the Goryeo royal house would intermarry with Mongol princesses. Goryeo was reduced from a vassal to a wholly dependent state, though the Goryeo kingship was preserved. The Yuan dynasty controlled Goryeo through intermarriage and intervention in internal affairs.

Hulagu formally established the Ilkhanate in Persia, with its capital at Tabriz. The Ilkhanate was one of the four major khanates of the Mongol Empire, encompassing present-day Iran, Iraq, Azerbaijan, Armenia, Georgia, and western Afghanistan. Hulagu styled himself Ilkhan ('subordinate khan'), nominally recognizing the suzerainty of the Great Khan in Yuan China, but ruling independently.

Simon de Montfort summoned another parliament, which for the first time formally included knights from the shires and burgesses from the towns. Although Montfort was later killed at the Battle of Evesham, the precedent he set was continued by Edward I. In 1295, Edward I summoned the 'Model Parliament', establishing the fixed pattern of the English parliament.

In the mid-13th century, Chinese compass technology reached Europe via Arab merchants. Sailors in the Italian ports of Amalfi and Genoa were among the first in Europe to use the magnetic compass for navigation. The compass transformed European seafaring, allowing ships to determine direction even on cloudy days or in fog, and no longer forced to sail along coastlines. The 1281 account of the Italian missionary Giovanni da Montecorvino mentions the Chinese use of the compass. The spread of the compass provided key technology for the Age of Discovery in the 15th century.

Charles of Anjou, brother of King Louis IX of France, at the invitation of Pope Clement IV, led an army south into Italy and defeated Manfred of Hohenstaufen. Manfred was killed at the Battle of Benevento, and Charles was crowned King of Sicily. He then conquered Naples, establishing the Angevin dynasty there (1266-1282).

The English Franciscan Roger Bacon, at the request of Pope Clement IV, completed his major works: 'Opus Majus', 'Opus Minus', and 'Opus Tertium'. In these works, Bacon advocated experimental scientific method and criticized the dogmatism and empty debates of scholastic philosophy. He also studied optics, alchemy, and astronomy, and speculated about the possibility of eyeglasses, telescopes, and steam-powered devices.

Emperor Kublai ordered the establishment of the Directorate of Imperial Academy (Guozijian) in the capital Dadu as the highest educational administration. The former Jin national academy was expanded into a proper school. The Guozijian taught Mongol, Semu, and Han Chinese students, covering Confucian classics, Mongolian language, archery and horsemanship. It aimed to cultivate ruling elites loyal to the Yuan and promote cultural exchange among ethnic groups.

Kublai decided to move his capital from Kaiping to Yanjing (present-day Beijing) and ordered the construction of a new city, Dadu, in the northeast of the former Jin capital of Zhongdu. The design was by the Han Chinese Liu Bingzhong, featuring a north-south axis and a triple structure of outer city, imperial city, and palace city. Dadu had a perimeter of 28.6 km and 11 gates, making it one of the grandest cities in the world at that time.

The Ilkhanate and the Mamluk Sultanate went to war over control of Syria. The Mamluk sultan Baibars I defeated the Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia and the Principality of Antioch, vassals of the Ilkhanate, in northern Syria, further strengthening Mamluk rule in the Levant.

v.t: St. Mark's Basilica in Venice rebuilt
v.s: In 1267, St. Mark's Basilica in Venice underwent extensive renovation and expansion. The church's Byzantine style and golden mosaics (the 'Golden Church') were essentially finalized. The work included adding domes and marble facade decoration.

Conradin, the last male of the Hohenstaufen dynasty, marched south into Italy to recover Sicily and Naples, but was defeated by Charles of Anjou at the Battle of Tagliacozzo. Conradin was captured and beheaded in Naples at the age of 16. The Hohenstaufen line was extinguished, and the Holy Roman Empire entered an even more chaotic Great Interregnum.

On the advice of his Han Chinese ministers, Kublai established the Censorate as the central supervisory organ, responsible for impeaching officials, monitoring administration, and advising the emperor. The Censorate had officials such as the Censor-in-Chief and Vice Censor-in-Chief, with subordinate agencies including the Investigation Bureau and the Palace Bureau. It was an important part of the Yuan's centralized system and helped curb official corruption.

In 1268, King Louis IX of France began preparations for the Eighth Crusade. Louis had been captured during the Seventh Crusade (1248-1254), but he was still determined to crusade again. The original target was Syria, but after events in Sicily he turned to Tunis. Louis invested heavily in building a fleet and army. In 1270 the French landed at Tunis, but plague broke out in the camp, and Louis himself died of the disease. The Eighth Crusade ended in failure.

v.t: War between Kublai and Kaidu breaks out
v.s: Kaidu, grandson of Ögedei, refused to recognize Kublai as Great Khan and, allied with the Chagatai and Golden Horde khanates, raised a rebellion in northern Xinjiang. Kublai sent a punitive expedition, and the two sides fought near Beshbalik (present-day Jimsar, Xinjiang). The war lasted thirty years (1269-1301), severely depleting Yuan resources and causing Xinjiang (the Western Regions) to slip beyond Yuan control.

Sent by Kublai as envoys to the pope, Niccolò and Maffeo Polo returned to Europe carrying letters from Kublai requesting the pope send a hundred missionaries to China. They arrived at Acre in 1269, learned that Pope Clement IV had died and a new pope had not yet been elected, so they returned to Venice to wait.

v.t: Sakya school becomes state religion of Yuan
v.s: In 1269, Kublai Khan granted the title 'Imperial Preceptor' (Dishi) to the Sakya lama Drogön Chögyal Phagpa, making the Sakya school the state religion of the Yuan dynasty. Phagpa created the 'Phags-pa script' (Mongolian new script), which Kublai promulgated throughout the empire. With Yuan support, the Sakya school held both religious and political power in Tibet, establishing the Sakya regime and beginning the theocratic system in Tibet.

King Louis IX of France launched the Eighth Crusade, landing in Tunis, North Africa, in an attempt to force the Hafsid dynasty to convert to Christianity. However, plague struck the crusader army, and Louis himself died. His son Philip III succeeded and immediately withdrew. The Eighth Crusade ended in failure; it was the last major crusade of the Middle Ages.

Yekuno Amlak of Ethiopia, with the support of the Christian church, overthrew the Zagwe dynasty and established the Solomonic dynasty. This dynasty claimed descent from the Queen of Sheba and King Solomon, restoring 'Solomonic blood' rule. The Solomonic dynasty ruled Ethiopia until 1974, making it the longest-reigning dynasty in African history.

To develop agricultural production, Kublai established the Directorate of Agriculture, responsible for managing agriculture, sericulture, and water conservancy. The directorate compiled the 'Nongsang Jiyao' (Essentials of Agriculture and Sericulture), summarizing pre-Yuan agricultural techniques and promoting them nationwide. This was the first official comprehensive agricultural treatise in Chinese history and played a positive role in restoring northern agriculture.

v.t: Kublai changes state name to Yuan
v.s: Following the advice of Liu Bingzhong, Kublai changed the state name from 'Great Mongol State' to 'Great Yuan', taken from the 'Book of Changes' phrase 'Great is the Qian, the primal.' The establishment of the Yuan dynasty marked Kublai's transformation from a steppe nomadic empire to a Chinese-style dynasty. Kublai proclaimed himself Emperor Shizu of Yuan and posthumously honored Genghis Khan as Emperor Taizu of Yuan. The Yuan dynasty was the first national unified dynasty in Chinese history established by a minority ethnic group.

v.t: Marco Polo departs for the East
v.s: The 17-year-old Marco Polo, together with his father Niccolò and uncle Maffeo, set out from Venice along the Silk Road to China. They carried the new Pope Gregory X's reply letter to Kublai and holy oil. The journey passed through Armenia, Persia, Afghanistan, the Pamirs, and the Taklamakan Desert, taking three and a half years, and they arrived at Yuan Shangdu in 1275.

Kublai issued the 'Zhiyuan Xinge', the first codified law of the Yuan dynasty, consisting of 20 chapters covering official ranks, households and marriage, finance, and penal law. The code integrated Mongol customary law and Central Plains legal traditions, forming the basis of the Yuan legal system.

v.t: Edward I crowned King of England
v.s: After Henry III died, his eldest son Edward I was crowned King of England (1272-1307). Edward I was a famous 'legislator' in English history; through the Statutes of Westminster, he expanded the power of parliament, restricted feudal privileges, and unified the English common law system. He also conquered Wales and attempted to conquer Scotland.

v.t: Yuan establishes Hanlin National History Academy
v.s: Kublai established the Hanlin National History Academy, responsible for compiling national history, drafting edicts, and producing imperial proclamations. The Yuan Hanlin Academy also undertook the compilation of the histories of the Liao, Jin, and Song dynasties, with Tuotuo presiding over the compilation of the 'History of Song', 'History of Liao', and 'History of Jin', making significant contributions to the preservation of Chinese historical documents.

In 1272, the Mamluk sultan Baibars captured some fortresses of the County of Tripoli (present-day northern Lebanon). Tripoli itself was captured by Sultan Qalawun in 1289. Acre, the last major crusader stronghold in the East, fell in 1291.

After a six-year siege, the Yuan army finally breached the city of Xiangyang. The Yuan used counterweight trebuchets (called 'huihuipao') brought from the west to collapse the walls, and the Song commander Lü Wenhuan surrendered when food and reinforcements ran out. Xiangyang was the key point of the Song's Yangtze River defense line; its fall signaled that the destruction of the Southern Song was inevitable.

v.t: Great Interregnum ends in Holy Roman Empire
v.s: The German princes elected Count Rudolf I of Habsburg as King of the Romans, ending the 19-year 'Great Interregnum' (1254-1273). Rudolf I recovered imperial lands that had been seized by the princes and defeated King Ottokar II of Bohemia, establishing the Habsburg family's dominance in Germany.

After completing the third part of the 'Summa Theologica', Thomas Aquinas suddenly stopped writing, saying, 'All I have written seems like straw.' In 1274, Aquinas died while traveling to the Second Council of Lyon. In 1323, Pope John XXII canonized him; in 1567, Pope Pius V conferred on him the title 'Angelic Doctor'. The 'Summa Theologica' became the highest authority in Catholic theology.

v.t: First Mongol invasion of Japan (Battle of Bun'ei)
v.s: Kublai sent a Mongol-Han-Korean army of about 40,000 under Xin Du and Hong Cha-qiu to invade Japan from the Korean Peninsula. The Yuan army landed at Hakata Bay in northern Kyushu, using gunpowder weapons and counterweight trebuchets against the Japanese. The Japanese, inexperienced against such weapons, initially suffered losses. However, a typhoon struck that night, destroying most of the Yuan fleet, forcing a retreat. The Japanese called the typhoon 'kamikaze' (divine wind).

Emperor Duzong Zhao Qi, due to long-term indulgence in wine and women, died in Lin'an at age 35. He reigned for ten years, during which court affairs were controlled by Jia Sidao, and the Song government was corrupt and the military neglected. After Duzong's death, Empress Dowager Xie established the three-year-old Zhao Xian as Emperor Gong. Jia Sidao continued to monopolize power, and the Song countdown to destruction began.

v.t: Franciscans allowed to preach in Beijing
v.s: In 1274 (the 11th year of Zhiyuan), Kublai Khan allowed Italian Franciscan friars to establish churches and preach in Dadu (Beijing). Previously, Kublai had sent envoys to Rome, and Pope Gregory X sent Franciscans like John of Montecorvino back to the Yuan court. The Franciscans built a church in Dadu and translated some Christian texts, coexisting with Nestorian Christianity. This was the first time Catholicism was formally allowed to spread in China's interior.

v.t: Marco Polo arrives at Yuan Shangdu
v.s: Marco Polo, together with his father and uncle, arrived at Yuan Shangdu (present-day Zhenglan Banner, Inner Mongolia) and was warmly received by Kublai. Marco was clever and quick to learn Mongolian and Chinese, winning Kublai's favor. He then lived in China for 17 years, holding posts such as Inspector of Yangzhou, and traveled to Hangzhou, Chengdu, Kunming, Quanzhou, and other places.

The Yuan commander Bayan led his army down the Yangtze and met the Song chancellor Jia Sidao's 130,000 Song troops at Dingjiazhou (present-day Tongling, Anhui). Jia Sidao fled the battlefield, and the Song army collapsed. The Yuan took advantage of their victory to capture Jiankang (present-day Nanjing), Zhenjiang, and other strategic points; the Song's Yangtze defense line completely collapsed. Jia Sidao was killed during his flight by the escort officer Zheng Hu Chen.

In 1267-1268, the English Franciscan Roger Bacon completed his major work, 'Opus Majus'. The treatise systematically discussed grammar, logic, mathematics, optics, experimental science, and moral philosophy. Bacon emphasized the importance of experiment and mathematics, advancing the idea that 'knowledge is power'. Because his ideas were too radical and he criticized the church, Bacon was imprisoned by the Franciscans for more than a decade in 1278.

The Yuan army approached the Southern Song capital Lin'an (present-day Hangzhou). Empress Dowager Xie and Emperor Gong submitted a surrender, and Lin'an surrendered without a fight. The Yuan army escorted Empress Dowager Xie, Emperor Gong, the Song imperial clan, and officials to Dadu. Kublai named Emperor Gong the Duke of Ying and later sent him to Tibet to become a monk. The Song's rule in Lin'an came to an end.

After the fall of Lin'an, loyal officials Wen Tianxiang, Lu Xiufu, and Zhang Shijie proclaimed the elder brother of Emperor Gong, Zhao Shi, as emperor in Fuzhou, known as Emperor Duanzong. Wen Tianxiang organized volunteer forces to continue the resistance, fighting in Jiangxi, Guangdong, and other places. Wen Tianxiang wrote the poem 'Crossing the Lingding Sea', leaving the immortal line: 'Since ancient times, no one has escaped death; leave a loyal heart to illuminate history.'

v.t: Basilica of St. Francis in Assisi founded
v.s: In 1228, Pope Gregory IX, after canonizing St. Francis of Assisi (1182-1226), ordered the building of the Basilica of St. Francis in Assisi. The church, built on an Umbrian hill, has two levels: the lower church in Romanesque style and the upper church in Gothic style. The upper church's frescoes, painted by Giotto and others, depict the life of St. Francis. The basilica was consecrated in 1253.

The Yuan army captured Guangzhou, and the Southern Song exiled court was forced to flee to the sea. Zhang Shijie escorted the fleet carrying Emperor Duanzong to Jing'ao (near present-day Macau), where a typhoon struck; the emperor fell into the water and died soon after from shock and illness. Lu Xiufu and Zhang Shijie then proclaimed Zhao Bing as emperor, the Last Emperor of Song.

Kublai sent Nasir al-Din to invade the Pagan Kingdom (present-day Myanmar). The Yuan army captured Jiangtou City (present-day Katha, Myanmar), and King Narathihapate of Pagan fled to Lower Burma. This was the beginning of the Yuan-Burmese war; subsequent Yuan invasions caused the Pagan kingdom to break up into smaller states.

In 1277, the theology faculty of the University of Paris condemned 219 philosophical and theological propositions (the 'Condemnations of 1277'). These propositions, drawn from Aristotelian philosophy and 'Latin Averroism', were seen by the church as threatening Christian orthodoxy. This event was one of the most significant intellectual censorship episodes of the Middle Ages.

Wen Tianxiang and his forces were surprised by a Yuan detachment under Zhang Hongfan while eating at Wupoling, Haifeng, Guangdong, and he was captured. Wen swallowed ice (camphor) in a failed suicide attempt and was taken to the Yuan camp. Zhang Hongfan urged him to surrender; Wen refused, writing the poem 'Crossing the Lingding Sea' to declare his loyalty. He was then sent to Dadu, where Kublai repeatedly tried to persuade him to surrender, but Wen remained steadfast.

King Rudolf I of the Holy Roman Empire defeated King Ottokar II of Bohemia at the Battle of Dürnkrut; Ottokar was killed. Rudolf recovered the imperial territories of Austria, Styria, Carinthia, and Carniola that Ottokar had seized, and granted Austria to his two sons. The Habsburg family thus became the most powerful princes in Germany.

v.t: Yuan establishes Penghu Patrol and Inspection Office
v.s: In 1278 (the 15th year of Zhiyuan), the Yuan dynasty established a Patrol and Inspection Office in the Penghu Islands, under the jurisdiction of Tong'an County, Quanzhou Route, Fujian Province. The office was the first formal administrative institution set up by the Yuan in the Taiwan Strait, responsible for managing merchant shipping, maintaining sea lane safety, and collecting shipping taxes. Its establishment marked the first time the Chinese central government had formally administered the Penghu area, setting a precedent for the Qing and Ming dynasties' governance of the Taiwan-Penghu region.

The Yuan naval commander Zhang Hongfan engaged the last naval forces of the Southern Song exiled court at Yamen (present-day Xinhui, Jiangmen, Guangdong). The Song commander Zhang Shijie linked his thousand-plus ships with iron chains to form a water fortress; the Yuan used fire attacks and surprise tactics to cut off Song supplies. The Song fought stubbornly but were overwhelmed. Seeing the situation hopeless, Lu Xiufu first drove his wife and children into the sea, then carried the eight-year-old Last Emperor Zhao Bing and jumped into the sea, drowning. Zhang Shijie also died in a typhoon after breaking out. Tens of thousands of Song officials, soldiers, and civilians perished. The Battle of Yamen marked the fall of the Southern Song and a major rupture in classical Chinese civilization.

Wen Tianxiang was imprisoned in Dadu for three years; Kublai sent former Song officials, Yuan officials, and Wen's younger brother to persuade him to surrender, but Wen remained unmoved. Kublai personally tried to persuade him, offering high office, but Wen replied, 'I received the favor of the Song dynasty as a chancellor; how could I serve two dynasties? I ask only to die.' In the end, after the Southern Song had already fallen, Wen Tianxiang still refused to surrender and was beheaded at Dadu's Caishikou at age 47. In prison he had written 'Song of the Righteous Spirit', which became a symbol of Chinese moral integrity.

In 1279 (actually 1277), Bishop Étienne Tempier of Paris, acting on authority from Pope John XXI, condemned 219 philosophical propositions. These propositions came from Latin Averroists such as Siger of Brabant and Boethius of Dacia, touching on double truth, the eternity of the world, and other issues.

After the Battle of Yamen, the remnants of the Southern Song were wiped out. The Yuan dynasty completed the reunification that had been absent since the end of the Tang dynasty, with a territory covering present-day China, the Mongolian plateau, southern Siberia, eastern Central Asia, the northern Korean Peninsula, and northern Myanmar. The Yuan dynasty was one of the largest in Chinese history and the first national dynasty established by a minority ethnic group.

The 'Shoushi Calendar', compiled by astronomers Guo Shoujing and Wang Xun, was formally promulgated. It set the year length as 365.2425 days, the same as the Gregorian calendar, but more than 300 years earlier. The 'Shoushi Calendar' used advanced mathematical methods such as arc-sector circular cutting and the finite difference method. It was one of the most accurate calendars in ancient China and remained in use until the Qing dynasty.

v.t: Flanders becomes European wool textile center
v.s: The region of Flanders (present-day Belgium and northern France) became the center of European wool textile production. Cities such as Bruges, Ghent, and Ypres imported English wool and processed it into high-quality broadcloths for export across Europe. The prosperity of the wool textile industry gave rise to the earliest capitalist elements in Europe and promoted urban self-government and financial development.

v.t: Second Mongol invasion of Japan (Battle of Kōan)
v.s: Kublai launched a second invasion of Japan, sending two armies: an Eastern Route army of 40,000 from Korea and a Southern Route (Jiangnan) army of 100,000 from Ningbo, totaling about 140,000 troops. The Yuan army landed at Hakata Bay in northern Kyushu; the Japanese had built stone walls along the coast to defend. The two sides fought for two months, when the Yuan fleet was again struck by a typhoon (kamikaze), losing most of its ships and half its men, forcing a retreat. Although Kublai wanted to try again, the empire's resources were insufficient, and he abandoned the plan.

The Turkish tribal leader Osman I succeeded his father Ertuğrul as chief of a small territory in northwestern Anatolia (present-day Bilecik, Turkey). He began raiding the Byzantine frontier and attracting ghazi warriors, laying the foundation for the future Ottoman Empire.

v.t: Ilkhanate and Mamluk Sultanate sign truce
v.s: In 1281, the Ilkhanate under Abaqa Khan and the Mamluk Sultanate under Qalawun fought the Battle of Homs in Syria. After the Mongol defeat, the two sides signed a ten-year truce, with the Ilkhanate recognizing Mamluk control over Syria.

In Palermo, Sicily, a riot broke out when a French soldier harassed a local woman during Easter vespers. The uprising quickly spread across the island; the Sicilians cried 'Death to the French!' and killed about two thousand Frenchmen. The rebels invited King Peter III of Aragon (whose wife was a Hohenstaufen descendant) to rule Sicily. Charles of Anjou and Aragon then fought a 20-year war for control of Sicily.

To administer Goryeo and prepare for an invasion of Japan, Kublai established the Zhengdong Branch Secretariat in Goryeo (a Yuan branch in the Korean Peninsula). The king of Goryeo served as the Left Chancellor of the branch secretariat, but real power was held by the darughachi (overseer) sent by the Yuan. The establishment of the branch secretariat marked Goryeo's transformation from a nominal vassal to a branch of the Yuan.

In 1282, the English Franciscan Roger Bacon, in his 'Opus Majus', recorded the earliest known European formula for black gunpowder (a mixture of saltpeter, charcoal, and sulfur). Bacon's knowledge likely came from Chinese accounts. However, European use of gunpowder weapons only began in the early 14th century.

After several years of war, King Edward I of England finally conquered Wales. He killed the Welsh prince Llywelyn ap Gruffudd and his brother Dafydd. Edward I then built more than ten castles in northern Wales, including Caernarfon, Conwy, and Beaumaris, to suppress Welsh resistance. In 1301, Edward I made his eldest son (later Edward II) Prince of Wales, a title that English heirs to the throne have held ever since.

Due to paper currency depreciation, the Yuan issued the 'Zhiyuan Paper Currency Law', printing new Zhiyuan notes and stipulating that one string of Zhiyuan would exchange for five strings of Zhongtong notes. However, the new notes also depreciated, and inflation continued to worsen. In the late Yuan, massive printing led to hyperinflation and economic collapse, contributing to the dynasty's downfall.

In 1283 (according to Dante; actually 1274), nine-year-old Dante first saw eight-year-old Beatrice Portinari at a May Day festival in Florence. This meeting became the lifelong source of Dante's emotions and literary creation. Beatrice later married someone else and died young (1290); Dante made her a guide of the soul in his 'Vita Nuova' and 'Divine Comedy'.

Kublai sent Prince Toghan (his ninth son) to lead an army against Annam (present-day northern Vietnam). The Yuan captured the Annamese capital Thang Long (present-day Hanoi), but the Annamese king Trần Nhật Duật, with the help of the famous general Trần Hưng Đạo (Prince Hưng Đạo), used scorched-earth and guerrilla tactics. The Yuan army was forced to withdraw due to lack of supplies and plague. The Yuan-Vietnam war continued until 1288, when Annam eventually agreed to pay tribute to the Yuan.

v.t: Pope Martin IV excommunicates King of Aragon
v.s: After the Sicilian Vespers, Pope Martin IV sided with Charles of Anjou and excommunicated King Peter III of Aragon, calling for a crusade against Aragon. However, the Aragonese, under Admiral Roger of Lauria, defeated the French fleet, and the papal crusade failed.

In 1284, the Republic of Venice completed its Venetian Code, systematically organizing maritime law, commercial law, and civil law. Venice, as a maritime trading republic, had a legal system that protected commercial contracts, shipping insurance, and partnerships. Venetian commercial practices later provided models for the commercial codes of continental Europe. The Venetian civil code and foreign affairs law were widely adopted in the Adriatic and eastern Mediterranean.

Toghan again led an army against Annam but was defeated again by Trần Hưng Đạo at the Battle of Bạch Đằng. Trần Hưng Đạo had driven pilings into the riverbed of the Bạch Đằng River and lured the Yuan fleet in at high tide; when the tide fell, the Yuan ships were impaled and nearly annihilated. Toghan fled back to Guangxi in disgrace. An enraged Kublai stripped Toghan of his title but did not launch another major campaign against Annam.

v.t: King Philip III of France dies
v.s: King Philip III of France (the Bold) died of illness during the Aragonese Crusade, and his son Philip IV (the Fair) succeeded. Philip IV was one of the most powerful monarchs in French history; through struggles with Pope Boniface VIII and the suppression of the Knights Templar, he greatly strengthened royal power.

In 1285, the English Franciscan John Pecham completed his theological work 'De Trinitate' (On the Trinity). Pecham served as Archbishop of Canterbury (1279-1292) and was an important representative of scholastic philosophy. His works mainly concerned the doctrine of the Trinity and the sacraments. Although less influential than his contemporaries Thomas Aquinas and Duns Scotus, as head of the English church he played an important role in theological education and church reform.

King Alexander III of Scotland died after a fall from his horse; his only direct heir, his granddaughter Margaret (the Maid of Norway), was only three years old. Scotland fell into a succession crisis, and King Edward I of England, claiming the right of guardianship, intervened in Scottish affairs, attempting to bring Scotland under English control, which eventually sparked the Scottish Wars of Independence.

v.t: Yuan establishes Regional Censorate in Jiangnan
v.s: Kublai established a Regional Censorate in Jiangnan (south of the Yangtze), alongside the existing Censorate in Dadu. The Southern Censorate was responsible for supervising local officials in the Jiangnan region, strengthening central control over the south. This was an important measure to consolidate Yuan rule over southern China.

Around 1136, Geoffrey of Monmouth published 'Historia Regum Britanniae', the first systematic account of King Arthur and the Knights of the Round Table. In the 13th century, French writer Chrétien de Troyes wrote the stories of Arthur and the Holy Grail. In 1267, Thomas Malory's 'Le Morte d'Arthur' synthesized all the legends in English. The Arthurian legends became the central theme of medieval chivalric literature.

Around 1286, glassmakers in Venice or Florence invented the first wearable convex lens glasses to correct presbyopia. A 13th-century sermon mentions 'glass lenses for the elderly'. The invention of eyeglasses greatly extended the working life of scholars and fueled the academic prosperity of the Renaissance. Over the following centuries, glass production and design continued to improve.

The Yuan army, taking advantage of the Pagan kingdom's internal weakness, captured the city of Pagan (present-day Bagan, Myanmar). The last Pagan king, Narathihapate, fled to Lower Burma and was killed. The Pagan dynasty (1044-1287), which had lasted 240 years, was destroyed. Northern Burma came under Yuan control, while the south split into several small states such as Pegu and Ava.

The Italian poet Dante first saw Beatrice Portinari, the love of his life, on a bridge in Florence. Although Beatrice later married someone else and died young at 24, she became Dante's spiritual guide and symbol of love. In the 'Divine Comedy', Beatrice guides Dante through Paradise.

In 1287 (the 24th year of Zhiyuan), the Yuan dynasty formally established Yunnan Province, with its capital at Zhongqing (present-day Kunming), and governed through routes, prefectures, counties, and other administrative units. Before this, Yunnan was mainly controlled by the Dali kingdom and local chieftains. After Kublai pacified Dali in 1253, the Yunnan Branch Secretariat was set up. The establishment of the province strengthened central government control over the Yunnan frontier, promoted Han Chinese migration into Yunnan, and facilitated economic development and cultural exchange among ethnic groups.

The Yuan dynasty's third campaign against Annam ended in defeat at the Bạch Đằng River by the Annamese general Trần Hưng Đạo. The Annamese had driven wooden stakes into the riverbed; the Yuan fleet was trapped when the tide fell, losing eight- or nine-tenths of its ships. An enraged Kublai was persuaded by his ministers to stop the campaign. Annam agreed to pay tribute to the Yuan once every three years, maintaining a tributary relationship.

The young Queen Margaret of Scotland died en route to Scotland, extinguishing the royal line. King Edward I of England, acting as arbitrator, selected John Balliol from among 13 claimants to be king. However, Balliol was soon forced to abdicate under Edward's pressure, and Edward I directly annexed Scotland.

In 1235-1239, the Mongol Empire launched a third large-scale invasion of Goryeo. The Mongols had previously invaded twice (1231-1232), forcing the Goryeo court to move from Kaesong to Ganghwa Island to resist. In the third invasion, the Mongols used a 'scorched earth' tactic, pushing deep into Goryeo and destroying most towns, causing widespread destruction. Although the Goryeo court held out on Ganghwa Island, the economy was devastated. In 1241 Goryeo sued for peace, agreeing to send a royal prince as hostage, but the Mongol demand that the Goryeo court leave Ganghwa Island was not fulfilled until 1259.

The Mamluk sultan Qalawun led an army to capture the important Crusader port city of Tripoli (present-day Tripoli, Lebanon). The defending Knights Hospitaller and residents resisted fiercely but were overwhelmed. The County of Tripoli (1109-1289) was destroyed; this was the last major Crusader stronghold in the Levant.

On the advice of Guo Shoujing, Kublai ordered the excavation of the Tonghui River to connect Dadu with Tongzhou (present-day Tongzhou district, Beijing). The Tonghui River, more than 160 li long, drew water from the White Floating Spring in Changping and various springs in the Western Hills, solving Dadu's grain transport problem. Southern grain could now reach Dadu's Jishuitan directly via the Grand Canal, greatly reducing reliance on maritime transport.

In 1289, the Republic of Florence issued the 'Ordinances of Justice', by which the seven major guilds (the Calimala cloth merchants, bankers, wool merchants, etc.) took control of the city's government. The guild system made Florence the first city-republic in Europe controlled by the bourgeoisie. Officials were chosen by sortition. Guild rule drove Florence's economic prosperity and the rise of Renaissance culture.

King Edward I of England issued the Edict of Expulsion, ordering all Jews to leave England by 1 November. Jews had lived in England for about 200 years, primarily as moneylenders. Using the pretext of usury, Edward I expelled them and confiscated their property. Jews were not allowed to return until the Cromwellian period in 1655.

v.t: Yuan builds Altars of Soil and Grain
v.s: Kublai built altars for the gods of soil and grain in the southern suburbs of Dadu, following Han custom to worship the gods of earth and grain. This move marked the Yuan rulers' further acceptance of traditional Central Plains ritual culture, helping to ease Han-Mongol ethnic tensions.

Between 1250 and 1290, the west facade, north and south transept facades, and the famous rose windows of Notre-Dame de Paris were completed. The cathedral, begun in 1163, is a masterpiece of French Gothic architecture. The north and south rose windows measure 13 meters in diameter and are made of stained glass depicting biblical stories. The architectural features of Notre-Dame (flying buttresses, pointed arches, rib vaults) became standard for European Gothic cathedrals.

The three forest cantons of Uri, Schwyz, and Unterwalden swore an oath of alliance at the Rütli meadow on Lake Lucerne, signing the 'Federal Charter of 1291'. The charter stipulated mutual defense in case of attack, the preservation of autonomy, and the rejection of foreign judges. The year 1291 is therefore considered the founding year of the Swiss Confederation. The confederation gradually expanded and later became a permanently neutral country.

The Mamluk sultan Al-Ashraf Khalil (son of Qalawun) led a large army to besiege Acre (present-day Akko, Israel), the last Crusader stronghold in the Levant. The Knights Hospitaller, Knights Templar, and Teutonic Knights defended for six weeks but eventually fell due to starvation and lack of relief. The Grand Master of the Templars and most of his members were executed. The Crusader states were completely destroyed.

v.t: Yuan establishes Directorate of Water Conservancy
v.s: Kublai established the Directorate of Water Conservancy, with Guo Shoujing as its head, to manage water conservancy throughout the empire. The directorate organized dredging of the Yellow River, canals, and rivers, and built many irrigation projects, promoting agricultural development under the Yuan.

v.t: Scottish War of Independence breaks out
v.s: After Edward I deposed John Balliol, Scottish nobles rose up against English rule. Edward I led an army into Scotland, capturing the southern border towns. The Scots sought help from France, forming the 'Auld Alliance' (1295) against England. The First War of Scottish Independence officially began (1296-1328).

After 17 years in the Yuan court, Kublai sent Marco Polo to escort the Mongol princess Kökötchin to marry Arghun Khan of the Ilkhanate. Marco Polo led a fleet of 14 ships from Quanzhou, sailing via Sumatra and India, and arrived in Persia after two and a half years. He then returned to Venice in 1295.

The Yuan army's expedition to Java was used by a local prince, Raden Wijaya, to defeat his rivals; Wijaya then turned on the Yuan and drove them out, establishing the Majapahit Empire. Under the leadership of Prime Minister Gajah Mada, Majapahit later unified the Indonesian archipelago, becoming the most powerful empire in Southeast Asia.

Kublai sent Shi Bi, Yi Hei-mi-shi, and Gao Xing with 20,000 troops to invade Java. After landing, the Yuan army exploited the rivalry between the kingdom of Singhasari and Kediri, helping the Javanese prince Raden Wijaya defeat his enemies. However, Wijaya then turned on the Yuan, inflicting heavy losses and forcing a retreat. Java subsequently established the powerful Majapahit Empire.

The Provençal troubadour Bertran de Born died. He was one of the most famous troubadours of the Middle Ages, known for his poems on war and chivalry. Dante placed him in the eighth circle of Hell in the 'Divine Comedy' for fomenting discord between father and son.

In 1293, the Republic of Florence minted the first gold florin. Each florin contained about 3.5 grams of fine gold (24 karat) and was about 19 mm in diameter. The florin quickly became the standard currency for international trade in Europe, trusted for its stable purity. The issuance of the florin solidified Florence's position as a financial center in Europe.

The founding emperor of the Yuan dynasty, Kublai Khan, died at the Zitan Palace in Dadu at age 80. He reigned for 35 years, completed the unification of China, established the Yuan dynasty, and implemented the provincial system and paper currency. After his death, his grandson Temür (son of Zhenjin) succeeded him as Emperor Chengzong. Temür ended large-scale external campaigns and adopted a policy of recuperation.

The cardinals elected Benedetto Caetani as pope, who took the name Boniface VIII. Boniface VIII was ambitious, trying to restore papal supremacy in secular affairs. He came into sharp conflict with King Philip IV of France over taxation and jurisdiction, leading to an open struggle between the papacy and the crown.

In 1248, Cologne Cathedral was founded. In 1294, the eastern choir, including the ambulatory and radiating chapels, was completed and consecrated. The choir, 42 meters high, is a masterpiece of German Gothic architecture. However, because of funding shortages, construction on the rest of the cathedral was interrupted; it was not finished until 1880, following the original design.

Marco Polo returned to his native Venice after 25 years in the East. During the war between Venice and Genoa, he was captured and in prison dictated his travels to the writer Rustichello, who recorded them as the book 'The Travels of Marco Polo' (also known as 'Il Milione'). The book detailed the wealth, prosperity, and civilization of China, greatly stimulating European desire for the East and indirectly fostering the later Age of Discovery.

To raise money for wars against Scotland and France, King Edward I of England summoned the famous 'Model Parliament'. This parliament included not only nobles and bishops but also two knights from each shire and two burgesses from each borough. The Model Parliament established the principle of representation in the English parliament and became a model for future parliaments.

v.t: Marriage alliance between Yuan and Ilkhanate
v.s: In the first year of Yuanzhen (1295), the Yuan dynasty and the Ilkhanate reinforced their suzerain-vassal relationship through marriage. Emperor Chengzong Temür gave a Mongol princess in marriage to Ilkhan Ghazan, recognizing Ghazan as the legitimate Ilkhan. Ghazan (r. 1295-1304) converted to Islam and carried out reforms, but maintained a tributary relationship with the Yuan. The alliance ensured the security of the northern Silk Road, facilitating frequent trade and diplomatic missions between East and West; travelers such as Marco Polo used this route to travel between Europe and Asia.

Alauddin Khalji, the second sultan of the Khalji dynasty in the Delhi Sultanate, ascended the throne (1296-1316). He was one of the most powerful rulers of the Delhi Sultanate, extending its territory through military conquests into southern India and repelling several Mongol invasions. He also implemented price controls and market reforms, stabilizing prices in the Delhi region.

King Edward I of England invaded Scotland, defeated the Scottish army at the Battle of Dunbar, and deposed King John Balliol. Edward I took the Scottish crown to London and appointed English officials to govern Scotland. The Scots, led by William Wallace and Andrew Moray, rose in revolt, and the Scottish War of Independence officially began.

In 1296 (actually after 1307), Dante, after being exiled, began his epic poem the 'Divine Comedy'. He was sentenced to perpetual exile in 1302 by his political opponents in Florence. During his exile, he wrote the 'Divine Comedy' in the Tuscan vernacular rather than Latin, describing an allegorical journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise.

Scottish rebel leaders William Wallace and Andrew Moray led a Scottish infantry army to ambush the English army at Stirling Bridge. Wallace attacked as the English were crossing the narrow bridge; the English commander, the Earl of Surrey, made tactical errors, leading to a crushing English defeat, with thousands killed or drowned. Wallace was subsequently named Guardian of Scotland. The battle was a major victory in the Scottish War of Independence.

King Philip IV of France took advantage of Count Guy of Dampierre's alliance with England to invade Flanders. The French army faced resistance from Flemish militias near Kortrijk, but ultimately gained control of the main Flemish cities. The textile-producing cities of Bruges, Ghent, and Ypres were forced to accept French rule.

In 1297, after defeating the English garrison at Lanark, Scottish hero William Wallace was formally knighted by Scottish nobles near Selkirk Forest and elected 'Guardian of Scotland'. In September of that year, Wallace defeated the English army at Stirling Bridge. However, Wallace was defeated by Edward I at Falkirk in 1298, captured in 1305, and executed.

Marco Polo's dictation in a Genoese prison was completed as 'The Travels of Marco Polo'. The book, divided into four volumes, gives a detailed account of Marco Polo's travels in China, Southeast Asia, India, and Persia, with particularly stunning descriptions of Kublai's court, Dadu (Beijing), Hangzhou, Quanzhou, and other cities. The accounts of China's wealth and civilization later inspired explorers like Columbus to seek new sea routes to the East.

King Edward I of England, with a large army, defeated the Scottish rebel army led by William Wallace at Falkirk (present-day central Scotland). Edward used heavy arrow fire from longbowmen to break through the Scottish schiltron formations; the Scottish cavalry fled, and Wallace escaped to France. Wallace's guardianship was abolished, and the Scottish independence movement suffered a temporary setback.

v.t: Yuan prohibits private possession of weapons
v.s: In the second year of Dade (1298), Emperor Chengzong Temür issued an edict strictly forbidding private possession of weapons, including bows and arrows, swords and spears, armor, and firearms. Possessed weapons had to be handed over to the authorities by a set deadline, with severe penalties for violators. As a minority Mongol regime, the Yuan implemented strict weapons control in Han Chinese areas, reflecting its insecurity. However, the prohibition did not prevent the Red Turban Rebellion and other uprisings.

Osman I led his forces to defeat the Byzantine army in Anatolia, capturing Karacahisar ('Black Castle') and making it his capital. He declared independence from the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum, no longer recognizing its suzerainty. This marks the foundation of the Ottoman Empire (1299-1922). Osman and his successors expanded through holy war, eventually destroying the Byzantine Empire and becoming a vast empire spanning three continents.

Emperor Chengzong sent Haishan (later Emperor Wuzong) on a western campaign, defeating the rebel Kaidu in the Altai Mountains. Kaidu fled to Central Asia and died soon after; his son Chapar succeeded him. The war between the Yuan and Kaidu's son continued until a peace agreement in 1304.

v.t: Capital of Golden Horde, Sarai, completed
v.s: In 1299, Sarai, the capital of the Golden Horde (Kipchak Khanate), was completed and reached its peak. Sarai was founded by Batu and became one of the largest cities in Europe (population about 100,000), with brick and stone buildings equipped with heating and running water, as well as mosques and palaces. Sarai was the political and commercial center of Mongol-Tatar rule over Russia and a key northern hub of the Silk Road. It was destroyed by Timur in 1395 and declined.

The Turkish chieftain Osman I declared the independence of his state. Over the following centuries, the Ottoman Empire expanded constantly, capturing Constantinople in 1453, reaching its peak in the 16th century, and controlling Eastern Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa.

In Florence, political factions Black (supporting the pope) and White (opposing papal interference) clashed; Dante belonged to the Whites. Pope Boniface VIII sent Charles of Valois, the brother of the king of France, into Florence to support the Blacks. After the Blacks seized power, Dante was sentenced in absentia to exile on charges of embezzlement and opposing the pope. Dante never returned to Florence thereafter, and wrote the 'Divine Comedy' in exile.

v.t: Great Zimbabwe civilization at its peak
v.s: Great Zimbabwe (present-day Zimbabwe) reached its peak. The city, built of stone, comprises the Hill Complex, the Great Enclosure, and the Valley Complex, with walls up to 11 meters high. Great Zimbabwe was the political and religious center of the Shona people, controlling the gold and ivory trade between the African interior and the Indian Ocean. This civilization represents one of the highest achievements of medieval African architecture and society.

v.t: Pope Boniface VIII proclaims first Jubilee
v.s: Pope Boniface VIII declared 1300 the first 'Holy Year' (Jubilee), promising a plenary indulgence (remission of all temporal punishment) to Christians who undertook a pilgrimage to Rome. Hundreds of thousands of pilgrims flocked to Rome, bringing the papacy enormous wealth and political prestige. The Jubilee tradition was continued by subsequent popes and remains an important Catholic observance.

v.t: Peak of Gothic art in Europe
v.s: Gothic art reached its peak in Europe, centered on Paris and radiating to England, Germany, Spain, and elsewhere. Gothic painting is represented by stained glass windows and illuminated manuscripts, with slender lines and bright colors; Gothic sculpture features lifelike figures with natural drapery. Cathedrals built in this period, such as Strasbourg Cathedral, Cologne Cathedral (subsequent phases), and Westminster Abbey, are masterpieces of Gothic architecture.

v.t: Three-field system fully replaces two-field system in Europe
v.s: Around 1300, European peasants widely adopted the three-field system, dividing arable land into spring-sown, autumn-sown, and fallow sections rotated annually. This system increased agricultural output by about 33% compared to the two-field system, reducing fallow time. Its spread was a core element of the medieval agricultural revolution, providing the food base for population growth and urban revival in Europe.
